sections.name: New Glasgow
sections.description:
Très belle section pour s'initier au creek ;)

**Water level**

Le niveau est pris en aval et donc une pondération de 0,26 s'applique . Par exemple, le niveau optimal est de 25cms au putin qui est égal à 96cms sur les données hydrométriques CEHQ.

Great section to get started on creeking. \
You put in at the end of the third little road on the right on Lavertu. That would be 125 Rue Lavertu, Saint-Calixte, QC J0K 1Z0.\
Be nice with the local owners.

Starts with a continuous II-III 1km long rapid.\
After the bridge and an R3, right after a flat section with properties on the right, the 15m cascade can be walked either side, but left is preferable as you stay away from private yards. At levels below 17cms, this cascade gets really bony. In the spring, the pool at the bottom on the right side has ice undercut to stay clear from.

After the golf and the hairpin turn to the left, you get to a washbowl with a strong recycle. Walk on river left or run it left to skim the hole. At higher levels, this hole runs nicer. A little lower, you pass left of a big rock and on toward the last rapid of the run. This one creates a river wide hole around 40cms, ending in a pool.

sections.name: Main
sections.description:
Nice spring river section for beginners.\
The [CCKEVM](https://www.cckevm.org) club organises a run on the Boulé/Archambault/Diable B every spring now in early May.

Drops into the Diable river around the intersection of the A and B sections.\
Continue into the Diable B for a fun longer run, which is a little bit more difficult.

The Boulé drops into the Archambault. Then, there is a dangerous rapid/cascade a few hundred meters before the indicated Archambault put-in. Best to exit at the prescribed Boulé take-out and portage or shuttle the boats to the prescribed Archambault put-in. This put-in is on private land. Please, consult the owner before using it.

The Archambault contributes around 50% to the Diable B, with the main contribution coming from the nice A section of the Diable. Diable stands for Devil in French.

<https://qcrivers.blogspot.com/2007/10/archambault-diable.html>
sections.flowsText: At around 48 on the Diable gauge, the flow is perfect. At 90 on that gauge, the section is still very manageable.

sections.name: Deux Chutes
sections.description: Nice smooth and easy run( II-III) with two waterfalls. One needs to be walked and the other can be run at low level (IV). Really long descent if you do the whole 16km, but you can park on the side of the road 4km down the river to have a smaller run of only 12km. (Look for the sign Camping Pont L'Assomption).

sections.name: Les Rentiers
sections.description:
Belle section pour débutant. Assez courte pour pouvoir la faire à toute heure de la journée. La section commence avec de court rapides de classe 1/EV. On atteint ensuite un bassin sous une ligne électrique qui annonce le début du rapide classe III ainsi que du seuil III subséquent. Le rapide se fait bien centre gauche. Le seuil est une courte cascade avec quelques vagues et finit dans un bassin. Un court plat nous amène à un magnifique rapide de classe II d'environ 600m. Ensuite quelques petites eaux-vives/R1 nous amènent à une section sans rapide qui marque la fin de la descente.

Faire attention à s'arrêter avant le pont, car un ancien barrage dangereux (s4) peut représenter un danger pour les pagayeurs inexpérimentés.

sections.name: Sept Chutes
sections.description:
This is a nice drop/pool run where you can bring beginners at normal or low flow.\
Rapids are short and easy to walk.\
The last rapid is the Rentier waterfall with a drop around 3.5m. Good waterfall for learning purposes.

A mostly flat start takes you to an island, normal run is on the left side of that island with a class 3 drop.\
Be careful of the siphon at the end of the rapid against the rocks of the island where it makes a left facing corner. The siphon is not very strong but still causes a lot of no so pleasant swims to the unaware paddler.

Note that there is a portage 3/4 of the way down the section, for a 4m double drop named chute au Cimetière de bois, right after a pool; portage is on river left.\
This drop has been run a few times in the right conditions - class V drop.

Blog de Fred Menage : <https://qcrivers.blogspot.com/2007/10/lassomption-section-des-7-chutes.html>

Put in : Parking on the side of the road, some resident have space on their front yard and allow to use as parking if asked.

Take out : There is an abandoned house (civic number is 350)that can be use as parking, make sure to respect the 30km/h. speed limit when you get there and to keep the spot clean as residents gently tolerate our presence.

sections.name: Lac Brompton
sections.description:
Les Sentiers de l’Estrie ont un sentier bien balisé qui suit le canyon rive gauche. Il permet de portager le canyon si désiré.\
La difficulté principale est un long R3-4 coulant dans le canyon d'au moins 600m, suivi par une longue section de R2-3 puis du R2.

Il y a une règle de niveau d'eau au point de mise l'eau, sous le pont en aval du barrage. Il faudrait donc aller sur place avec une photo repère au put-in. Si vous le faites, envoyez nous la photo.

Pour le moment, le minimum bas semble être quand le tuyau d’over-flow du barrage est complètement sous l’eau. Voir la section média pour une photo représentative.

sections.name: Sainte-Adèle
sections.description: The Aux Mulet is a tributary to the Du Nord and takes its source from lake Théodore and runs at the base of Mount Chanteclerc. Located in the heart of the Laurentides, this is a great spring run at high level, reserved for experienced paddlers. The river tends to be clogged by uprooted trees. Beware that, at high level, a few bridges will have to be walked for the lack of space under their arch.

sections.name: Lower
sections.description:
**Put In option 1**

Put after the the Sept iles water treatment plant on lac rapides. There is a short paddle perhaps 20min to the rapids of note. There is one short c2/3. The rapids of note in this upper section are two c4+ separated by a pool. The first is river left of the obvious island (get out and scout on the island), river right is mank. This drop is features a 10ish foot slide where most of the water hits the river right wall. Careful it is sharp. the second is a very fun boof into a fun canyon. There is one more rapid before Gros Chute. becare full of the hole on RL it is a sticky one. Portage Gros Chute RR. take out in the pool RL, or cary on down to the highway.

**Put in Option 2**

Bellow the gross chute (V) There are two rapids of note. The first (chute cran de fer) is a wonderfull 10 foot boof. the second is a longer gorge rapid. Both are class 4. This river can flood very quickly and will quickly turn both rapids into class V, if there has been a sustained amount of rain. you can easily scout the gorge rapid. park in the parking lot beside the river, and walk up the trail (river left). There is great camping on the river left, on the up river side of the highway the turn off to the spot is about 100m towards sept iles from the scout trail.

*By [Fraser Cepella](https://frasercepella.weebly.com/moisie-river.html?fbclid=IwAR1iv-Kd1wO3ouOB9RZLX0QyEV7V6mtsVPhxSZSMX8_vaKsrE9wsIYIIlJY)*

sections.name: CEPAL (slalom)
sections.description:
Well known CEPAL slalom course, once home of the 1979 worldwide championship.

Park your car at the downstream parking and hike up on the cycle path.

Gates are maintained by the CKRS kayak club who also holds competitions there every year. Please slide back the gates to shore after use since floods occurs on the river.

Level must be 20 m3/s or higher. Perfect level for slalom is 30-35 m3/sec. Over 100 m3/sec, the whole course becomes one big rapid with crashing diagonal.

The upper hole always runs, even at low level and is a good spot for playboating.

Rocks on the left bank are sharp.

sections.name: Bottom slides
sections.description:
The bottom section of the bear river is a lot of effort to get to, but it is well worth it for intermediate paddlers when at a good level. Put in at the Stonecliffe municipal boat launch and got up 9km to the mouth of the river by motorboat, as flatwater paddling this distance would be time consuming, and possibly draining for the actual paddling. Do not confuse the big bear with the little bear downstream of it, as their mouths look similar. Once in the right drainage, go up around 150m to the first slide. Getting out can be very tricky tho. There is a big log on shore beside a submerged rock shelf that you can walk on. Approach the lumber and tie your boat with a strap or an extra throw bag. Afterwards, walk your kayak through the water keeping your feet on that rock shelf all the way to the actual shore of the river. Hike your boat up around 250m and run the 5 nice slides to where your boat is parked. Repeat the lapping procedure until tired or out of time.

Happy paddling!

sections.name: Section A
sections.description:
Small creek just North of Montreal. Next to St-Jérôme. Drops into the Du Nord.\
First section starts at the bridge and ends at the little reservoir.\
Second section starts at that reservoir and ends at a sharp right curve in the woods of a new residential area being developed in 2019. Short 2 minutes walk from the river. This is the first creek to flow in the spring and the first to dry up.

There are no gauge for this creek; usually a percentage of the Du Nord, but not reliable as most rivers flowing into the Du Nord are still frozen when this one is at its prime flow.

There is a rock at the bottom of the right channel into the reservoir between the two sections, if it is covered and roosting, its minimum flow

[https://qcrivers.blogspot.com/2007/10/torrent-bellefeuille-laurentides.html](https://)

sections.name: Section B
sections.description:
Small creek just North of Montreal. Next to St-Jérôme. Drops into the Du Nord.\
First section starts at the bridge and ends at the little reservoir.\
Second section starts at that reservoir and ends at a sharp right curve in the woods of a new residential area being developed in 2019. This is the first creek to flow in the spring and the first to dry up.

There are no gauge for this creek; usually a percentage of the Du Nord, but this is not reliable as most rivers flowing into the Du Nord are still frozen when this one is at its prime flow.

There is a rock at the bottom of the right channel into the reservoir between the two sections, if it is covered and roosting, its minimum flow

With enough water, you can jump the left opening of the old dam into a jumble of rocks, but most walk this to the left. After that the difficulty ease up and the creek wanders into a nice wooded area, through angled rock slabs, up to the next hardest rapid at the old broken bridge. Regular line there is along the left side. Scout either left of right. Left also provides road access. At high water, the last slab before that rapid has retentive hydraulics. Right after the broken bridge, there is usually a hole that can be retentive at some levels. \
\
Back into wandering through the woods across shallow rock gardens, up to that sharp right curve where we exit. Short 2 minutes walk from the river to rue de l'Esplanade.
sections.flowsText: Experiemental: Switching to the new gauge on Bonniebrook. Help us tune the values.

sections.name: Betsiamites bomb
sections.description:
Multiple Gauges available from the station Bersimis-1, need to figure out which provides the flow in the section and then the range of levels / Plusieurs jauges sont disponibles à partir de la station Bersimis-1, il faut déterminer laquelle fournit le débit dans la section et ensuite la gamme de niveaux.

Some say this is the Stikine of the East,  very hard to get a good flow and most of the time it will be completely dry because of the dam upstream. You'll have to call hydro Quebec to figure out if it is in.

**Water levels**

80 - 120 cms: pool drop class V

300 - 400 cms: continues class V+ big water

sections.name: Blanche Perkins
sections.description:
Good to go after heavy summer flows or spring melts. This little section goes well with a the lower Blanche run which is also on the ww.guide app. It's also good if you want to run laps.
There are multiple pool drops and one large slide 70 foot slide with a pool drop.

Note: the large slide can be a back breaker at lower flows.

Some quality technical chutes and narrow runs. Also a nearly hole through a large culvert. This section has some unique features.

sections.name: Mayo
sections.description:
The Mayo Section is relatively short and is best suited with the Blanche Thurso run that is only 20 minutes away. Alternatively doing laps works just fine too.

This section is better the more water you have.  If the Rouge is at 100 cms and the Petite Nation is over 30cms this section is generally good to go.

It's  a small creek with tight little passages. The section has 5 rapids on the 4th rapid there will be a waterfall about 8 feet high, take it tight on the right and left boof to land with the current. The waterfall is easily lapped but be sure to say hi to the property owners. They are friendly and have been really great to kayakers.

Put in and take out from both bridges and you'll  be good. Be sure to talk to the locals if they're  checking you out while parking, let them know what your up too. They're generally cool with kayakers.

This section will be a great warmup for Blanche Thurso section down river.

Happy paddling

Ma Yo

Check my YouTube for other section guys, cheers

https://youtu.be/A-bqP816UxU

sections.name: Thurso
sections.description:
Fun intro to creeking located close to Ottawa/Gatineau. The upper half has larger rapids and some waterfalls that should be scouted very carefully. The bottom half of the river (from around highway 50 down) is all pool drop and has quite a few quality rapids. Most rapids are class III-IV with some exceptions which are easily portaged. This section normally runs for a few weeks in spring and sometimes fall - river tends to hold water for a while. \
\
Bonus section upsteam of this at Trou du Diable, normally good to go when the bottom line on gauge is just showing.

**Water levels**

No online gauge but generally when Petit Nation is over 80cms, it is good to go. 2 lines drawn on left side of dam (by bridge), bottom line is low runnable, upper line is a high flow (expect to portage a few).

**Alternative Put-In**

If you want to put in at the alternative put in its private property and you'll need to call ahead: Brian Maloney at Brylee Farms (819) 985-3882

Be sure to consider a steak or any other of the Brylee farms fine products. Be sure to give them a big thanks for continued access

<https://fermebrylee.ca/>
sections.season: spring or fall normally, comes in with heavy rains

sections.name: Familiale
sections.description:
Starts at Cime Aventure and ends at either Cime Aventure's takeout (river left) or the public takeout next to the port after the HW132 bridge.

Expect a 1.5- 2hr quiet paddle down a pristine river.

Cime Aventure will not help you with your own kayaks, but you can park your car at their location and take a taxi back up afterward.\
The local taxi (Taxi 132) can be reached at 581-364-8294 and the ride back up up should cost around 17$.

sections.name: Populaire
sections.description:
La Bonaventure est une de nos plus belles rivières à saumon.\
Elle offre une belle balade de fosse à saumon en fausse à saumon avec une eau d'une limpidité incroyable.

La mise à l'eau pour la petite descente «La Populaire» avec Cime aventure est au GPS 48°12'13.8"N 65°34'04.4"W. Le point de départ ressemble a un pit de gravier. Les images Google Maps sont vraiment claires, et en défilant le long de la rivière, on voit bien que c’est pas du classe V... Durant la descente, une petite rivière se jette dans La Bonaventure, ça peut être intéressant comme arrêt, des locaux y vont pour se baigner.  Point GPS 48°09'19.8"N 65°29'29.5"W.

Pas très loin du take out, il y a Le Malin, une belle petite vague, 48°06'00.5"N 65°27'40.8"W.  On peut y accéder en voiture, c’est un parc opéré par la municipalité.

Si vous êtes à Bonaventure quelques jours, visitez le [Bioparc de la Gaspésie](https://bioparc.ca/).

sections.name: La chute
sections.description:
La section commence par une chute située immédiatement en aval du pont du sentier de motoneige et bien visible depuis ce dernier.

Attention aux débris de pont potentiels et à la très faible profondeur sous la chute pour ceux qui seraient tentés de s'y risquer. Des blocs de béton et des tiges de fer d'anciennes structures sont visibles par endroits à l'eau basse.

Le rapide qui suit immédiatement la chute est un classe 3 avec roches coupantes et rouleau à rappel à éviter. Il se descend sur la droite dans sa section centrale.

Un seuil de 1m de dénivelé barre toute la rivière à la fin de ce rapide juste après le virage à droite. Plusieurs lignes sont possibles pour descendre ce seuil.

L'ensemble de la rivière est ensuite entrecoupée d'une dizaine de rapides de classe 2-3 relativement bien répartis entre des sections d'eau calme.

Attention aux anciennes structures de ponts sous l'actuel pont de chemin de fer au milieu de la descente. D'anciens pieux métalliques affleurent la surface à l'eau basse.

Bonne descente à tous.

Pour tout renseignements complémentaires contactez Eau Vive Expérience.

sections.name: Amont du pont
sections.description:
Series of short read-and-run creeking stretchs with just enough pool at the bottoms for fishing people and boats, perfect for initiating a good river runner paddler to creeking.

This is an high flow run, usually not doable before June since the put-in trail is packed with snow during the meltdown. It is a great section to be run during summer and fall when heavy rains occur and levels are high on the Valin. This section is never too high. It has been done with the Valin at 270m3/s...!!!

From the top, each rapid is almost blind. But once in, they are easy to read and run. The first one is the most difficult with a good hole in the center at 2/3 of the rapid, quickly followed by a sharp left turn caused by a huge boulder on the right. The other ones are pretty straight forward but fun.

Enjoy!

sections.name: Aval du pont
sections.description:
Easy, continuous but shallow section for beginners, except from the double drops waterfall at 1/3 of the descent.

Put in is at the bridge crossing the chemin Levesque. This run can be combined with the upper section.

All paddlers beginners and experts should be aware of that double drop, marked by an island, just after entering a small canyon.

Get out of your boat on the right side, cross the small channel and make the portage through the island.

\-Beginner-intermediate channel Channel on the right side of the island is runnable. It is a narrow with some sharp turns to make. For intermediate paddlers, it can be a way to avoid the crux on the left side of the island. Beginners will want to get in their boat at the end of that channel before the river merges again.

\-Advanced-expert channel
Left channel starts with a double drop with a huge kicker on the second one. Be aware that it is shallow at the bottom and it is preferable to run it with a minimum flow of 20 m3/s. Safety can be set up from the island by hiking down on a ledge.
If you don’t feel like doing the drops, you can get on that ledge and get back in your kayak a couple meters below the drops. There is a fun and not so easy canyon in front of you. Make sure to stay on the left as the canyon turns left.

Other than that, it is a fun river for beginners, with boulders and eddies to play with. There are many alternate parkings along the road if you wish to shorten the descent as the river becomes easier and easier as you are getting further away from the canyon.

Keep it quiet at the take out for the neighbors and please, don’t block the bridge !

sections.name: Amont du Testeur
sections.description:
This section of the Bras Louis is usually skipped by most paddlers seeking the challenge of the continuous RIV rock garden offered by the other the sections downstream. However, It could be a good intro to creeking for intermediate paddlers.

This is a relatively continuous section of R2 and R3 that gets interesting when the level is over (1) at the bridge marker. Below that level, you may lose more plastic than you would like to. All rapids are easy to read and run.

The take out (or put in for the Testeur and following sections) is at the sole shack on the right side of the river.

sections.name: Section A
sections.description:
Continuous section with over 3.5% slope, with little or no flat water offering limited safety at high water ( Valin over 80). It is preferable to be guided for your first time. Locals lap this is section in less than an hour.

If you are not planning to continue on Section B, be aware that the take out is very difficult to spot from the river and that there is a 10 minutes hike-out from there to your car.

sections.name: Section B
sections.description:
Do the Section A first then keep paddling to this more consequential B section.

There are four main features on this section.

\- A right turning nasty fall with a good portage from a tiny eddy on the right just on top of the fall.

\- The Funky Slide where all the water is being channeled to the top of a huge boulder from where the water is cascading down.

\- The Grand Prix with a hard entrance where we need to go left of a boulder against which all the water is pushing, followed by two holes just before a 40ft waterfall. Most people will want to get out on the tiny eddy on the left just before the fall.

\- The Fall itself. This fall is for expert only and beware of a huge hole at the very bottom.

sections.name: Testeur
sections.description:
This short R3-4 continuous stretch is called the Tester because it is a good indication for the rest and if you should get out at the bridge or keep paddling through the A/B sections.

Follow the main flow and enjoy.

sections.name: Section A
sections.description:
Very pretty section of class IV creeking in a nice decors.

Starts easy at the water station, where there is a scale.\
Walk the waterfall, river left. Get back in through a downward ravine ending at the bottom of the waterfall to run the exciting canyon.\
There is now a wooden ramp for launching.\
A set 3 or 4 rapids of class III/IV depending on the water levels will take you to a choice; either run/walk the big slide/cascade or go around into a small channel to the left to reach a fun narrow creek. Take the short slide and follow to the right to get to the entrance to that little creek. The creek turns to the left and down, then a through a short rock garden taking you back to the main flow.\
You can then walk back up to run the famous Toboggan.

If you head straight instead of opting for the little creek section, you get to the top of a large slide/cascade. First part is quite bony and many will walk that first part to hop back in for some spaced rock garden and then the well known S shaped rapid called Toboggan. Continue in the following rock garden to the junction with the little creek.

Continue down through a class III taking you to the last significant rapid of the section after a couple of large boulders on river right. This rapid is composed of a couple of ledges creating sizable hydraulics. There is a sneak pass a few feet from the left. A common route will follow a Z pattern starting right, then hard left to skim the 2nd line of hydraulics.

Some easy white water will take you to the exit back up the wooden stairs and up to the parking lot which is also used for Section B.\
Or continue down through Section B if the water level is sufficient.\
Shortly below the A take out, the Archambault joins the Diable from the left and bring in around 40% of the water going through the Diable B.

sections.name: Lower
sections.description:
To get to the put in: take chemain de l'areoport towards the Port Cartier correctional facility. Find the train tracks, park in an inconspicuous place. there is a dirt road before the tracks that works. If you take a right on that road you can scout the river again. From the train tracks it is a 3km walk (on/ beside the tracks till you get to the river) this is a very active train track, expect to see a train, there are no tunnels. when you hear the river, smash through the woods (100ish) to get to the river.

To get to the take out: take boulevard du portages des mousses, follow signs for camping municipale de paradis.

*By [Fraser Cepella](https://frasercepella.weebly.com/moisie-river.html?fbclid=IwAR1iv-Kd1wO3ouOB9RZLX0QyEV7V6mtsVPhxSZSMX8_vaKsrE9wsIYIIlJY)*

sections.name: Section B
sections.description:
Petit bijou des Laurentides à St-Adèle qui offre plusieurs beaux rapides drop/pool de classes III, IV et même V, dont 2 rapides plus longs en classe III+ et IV+. La seconde partie de cette section traverse le parc du même nom. Le canyon est souvent portagée car un bon trou est généralement présent au point où la rivière se déverse dans le canyon. Le Fer-à-Cheval est rétentif sauf à eau basse, mais une rampe sur sa gauche permet d'éviter celui-ci.\
Le parc offre un sentier sur les 2 rives et des toilettes au premier pont et une sortie de secours au parking du parc. Notez qu'il n'y a pas de frais de passage pour les kayakistes qui ne font que traverser le parc par la rivière.\
Au put-in, sur le Chemin des Hauteurs, les véhicules ne sont plus tolérés en stationnement sur l'accotement avec l'ajout de garde-fous des 2 côtés de la route. Attention à la circulation plus rapide sur cette ligne droite et aux riverains qui n'ont pas toujours été respectés dans le passé. 250m plus loin, il y a un espace pour le retournement des chasse-neige qui peut être utilisé pour stationner.\
Au take-out, attention pour ne pas bloquer les résidents. \
Le riverain Pierre Dumas, situé au 1545, Chemin des Rapides, permettait aux pagayeurs de traverser son terrain pour la sortie - longer la bordure droite jusqu'à des marches de pierre, puis monter l'entrée de son garage menant à la route. A chaque année, le CCKEVM lui fait parvenir une bouteille et un petit mot pour le remercier de sa gentillesse et de sa tolérance. Il semblerait que Pierre Dumas soit maintenant décédé, que sa fille ait repris la maison et qu'elle semble tolérer notre passage.

<https://qcrivers.blogspot.com/2007/10/doncaster-section-section-du-milieu.html>

*2024 update:* No more strainer Iron rods just above the bridge. 3.5 m3/s was -0.75 on the put in gauge

sections.name: Notre-Dame-de-la-Merci
sections.description:
Il roule lorsque la section Ouareau - Capucines est élevée.\
On peut partir de la halte routière à Notre-Dame-de-la-Merci. Le lac débouche sur une courte section roquailleuse peu profonde donnant sur une longue section d'eau calme avec un peu d'eau vive jusqu'à 4 seuils de classe IV/IV+, pour retourner au calme jusqu'à l'approche de la jonction Ouareau pour une dernière partie assez continue en III+.\
\
Trois options de mie-à-l'eau:\
\- Départ du lac de Notre-Dame-de-la-Merci.\
\- Départ juste en amont des chutes, plus ou moins +/- 4 km de la jonction avec la Ouareau\
\- Départ au Domaine Appaloosa pour la dernière partie plus soutenue et généralement combinée avec la section des Capucines.

sections.name: Below highway 50
sections.description:
Du Lievre is a waterfall paradise.

A section that comes in normally for a few days a year (when everything is at peak flows). Very close to Ottawa allowing after work laps. Note that rocks are very sharp in this section. There is very little flat water to recover and there are small bushes and trees in some parts of the section as the riverbed is dry most of the year. Hydro-Quebec is not very welcoming of kayakers either. Most park on the park on river left by the train tracks and hike up.

You should know that this section is owned by Hydro Quebec. Everyone and their dog in Buckingham uses this property as you will see the various paths along the river. However you should be aware that it is private property. You should also know that the section is a dam run off and there are trees throughout the section. A scout from the train bridge is mandatory (Dropped pin <https://maps.app.goo.gl/VS6bCzemyLu7UaC26>).

Lastly since the section is dam release and run off, a lot of the water runs into the dam and not necessarily the section or vice versa. 190 cms to 250 cms is a guideline for levels. In other words, a lot of water may or may not run into the dam so the gauge is not even close to perfect. Be your own judge. If the levels are about to change due to the dam a loud horn will sound and you should imperatively get off the river until you see the changes in water level.

Starting at the Rona or Tim Hortons parking  walk to the pin Dropped pin (<https://maps.app.goo.gl/Tup6P3SgyNkvEsMD8>)

There is a break in the chain link fence where one fence stops and another fence begins. Follow the path down to the river, you'll start in a pool below the dam. If the river splits and there is a choice in channels, keep left. Again a careful scout is necessary. There are other good channels but this guide is for the left channel. The majority of the run is solid Class 3+. The waterfall about half way is worth a second scout as the route is important; this part is at least a Class 4.  There will be some gnarly holes throughout but they all go at low to medium levels.

It is possible to park and play without running the top part.

Happy paddling Ma Yo

sections.name: Broken bridge to the Grand chutes
sections.description:
This is a very popular multiday canoeing trip on the Dumoine river. Although it usually takes canoeist 3-4 days to complete the journey, kayakers can do it comfortably with an overnighter when there’s decent flow in the river.

The paddling:

Directly at the put in, there’s a perfect warmup rapid, broken bridge. A few large waves, especially in high water where they can swamp a canoe and flip a kayak, lie ahead. Almost no holes lie throughout the whole rapids so just hit everything with speed. You will then get to a lake called Lake Benoit. This is where canoe trips use to begin when the plane shuttles were a thing. A few class I-II rapids exit the lake leading to Crampe en mass rapid. This has several fun eddies to catch and nice skip boofs in low water. The river then goes around an S band leading to a mandatory portage for canoes. FOR CANOES. It goes really well on the left through a nice tongue, as well as on river middle right where there’s a nice 3ft boof. The next rapid is Canoe eater. It goes well as long as you navigate the rock garden. Try not to flip or get pinned and watch beginners if they’re with you. This is the one rapid not to swim on the section. A few other substantial class II-III rapids follow like the Sleeper, Thread the Needle, and double choice which are all super straightforward in a kayak. Read and run. Eventually, you will get to a small rapid leading to a horizon line. Welcome to Little Steel chute. When there’s good flow, it’s a tongue onto a 6-8ft flake acting like a ramp. In lower water, the move is more consequential as of breaking a boat. If you’re in a canoe, this is a mandatory portage. The rest of the rapid goes on river right. It starts with a big hole. Paddle hard, lean forward, try to boof and you’re through. The rapids then wind down to a bunch of lakes. You will encounter the Sheerway and the Dumoine club along the way. Swifts and smaller rapids are in between the lakes. This is a good place to start looking for a campsite as there isn’t much action. A few kilometers of flatwater follow to Turner rapids until 8km later, The Grand chute is reached.

Don’t forget to organize shuttle with one of the local compagnies and pay your food/gas in the Swisha to support the local economy.

Happy paddling￼!

sections.name: Grand Chutes
sections.description:
Grand chutes is a series of about 7 chutes and rapids ranging from an R3 at the end to a solid R5 at the beginning.  If you're a solid r4 paddler you may want to take this challenge. You may consider skipping the first 2 falls however as the risks are high.

The rest of the rapids are mostly solid R4+ rock slides and or large rapids.

You'll  be entering the ZEC Dumoine, this is a Qc park association. Staying the night is a great option since you can camp right at the falls. The fees they charge are  very low.
You'll see a ZEC office upon entry of the ZEC you can pay before of after your stay.

Happy paddling,

Check out my [youtube channel](https://youtube.com/channel/UC_hauVMxONttWyp1ZvbC6EA) for video guides and other runs.

*Ma Yo*

At a certain water level, the very first set of falls and possibly the ones downstream become impossibly high. Probably this level would be around 80CMS

sections.name: Grand chutes to Ottawa river
sections.description:
This is a 2 to 3 day trip best suited for canoe camping.  However, if you do want to try the few chutes and waterfalls on your way down kayaking will work just fine too. There are many flats in between rapids.

You'll be entering ZEC Dumoine for this trip. You'll pay at the office on you're way in. If no one is manning the office just fill out the paper work and pay on your way out. The fees are minimal.

You'll  start out at grande chutes where there are many class 4 and 5 rapids.  If you're after class 3 stuff you'll be able to manage the last part after the chute. Put in at the eddy after the large rapid and navigate the rock garden.

During this trip you'll have plenty of warning before any chutes and all rapids have good portage trails. You won't need a map with your phone if you download the maps and GPS ahead of time. However Hap Wilson has written a section with great maps which can be purchased at MEC.

Other than the 3 chutes the whole section is full of very manageable class 1 to 3 rapids.  All easily scout able.

You'll  definitely want to check out pine valley campground to leave one of you cars for shuttle. There are a few other spots but pine valley is the most straight forward. 

The roads up to Grand chutes are quite good but continuing North from there is hazardous.  You would want to get a shuttle service. Parking at Grand chutes is also not a problem.

Be sure to buy a few meals in the Pontiac to support the local economy.

Happy paddling,
Ma Yo

Check out my you tube channel for more:

https://youtube.com/channel/UC_hauVMxONttWyp1ZvbC6EA

sections.name: A St-Jerome
sections.description:
Attention à la portion (après environ 1.5km de descente) qui débute du déversoir jusqu’à la grosse chute Wilson car c’est par endroit du classe 5+ (cascades qui s’enchainent). A plus haut niveau, il faudra être d’autant plus prudent. Dans cette portion, j’ai portagé par la rive gauche le déversoir/R5 au tout début et les chutes Wilson (chute et cascade la précédant: S5/6x2 sur la carte). Le S4/5 qui précède peut être fait mais il faut avoir conscience qu'une erreur peut nous mener très vite dans les chutes.

Au barrage désaffecté après la mi-parcours, il y a un passage à pied étroit rive droite pour portager. C’est assez difficile car il y a très peu d’espace et des vieux barbelés mais tout à fait possible. Le barrage a déjà été descendu en kayak par l'ouverture la plus à droite mais c'est très fortement déconseillé car inaccessible en cas de coincement (accident en 2017). Il serait intéressant de voir si un portage par la rive gauche serait plus facile.

Watch out for the dams, spillways and the rapids in the Wilson waterfall park, portaging is is highly recommended and often mandatory.

sections.name: Zec Dumoine
sections.description:
The Fildegrand river has some of the best whitewater in Eastern Canada according to the majority of people that have run it. A little bit after peak / high flows or in the fall/summer after a heavy rain, is the time to head for this one as peak flows on that river are extremely challenging. The boogie becomes class III-IV and the drops V+ and VI, meaning you will be walking a lot.

To get to the put in: Drive to Rapides-des-Joachims in Quebec and stop at the Dumoine zec office right after the bridge to register for road passes and sign up for recreation. Drive down main street and onto Dumoine road which you will follow until you cross another bridge. This is the registration shack for Zec Rapides-des-Joachims. Repeat the registration procedure, omitting the recreation signup. Drive up the main road until you get to the Grand chutes of the Dumoine. If you are doing the Fildegrand, this is way too high to even consider, although, it is a nice sight. Keep driving up the main road continuing straight after the Grand chutes for around 40km. When the road takes a slight left hand turn on a downhill, has a steep sand bank on the left, and a raving on the right, start watching for the trail entrance on the left side of the road. It is quite easy to miss! However, there is a sign that says Six mile dam at the entrance. Follow this trail until you get to the river. That is the put in.

Water levels: There is a visual gauge above the dam on river left.

If it is at 99.7, it’s minimum flow to run it. Perfect for intermediate-advanced boaters or just a mellow first time down. Most of the drops are mostly class IV with a few V’s at these flows. If the gauge reads at 99.6, it is too low.

***Note that the gauge is in a lake. Water going up by a single increment on the gauge in this very lake make a huge difference in power in some gorges and steep sections***

99\.8 means nice medium flows that could be considered by many prime flows. You are not really hitting rocks in the boogie sections, however, all drops and major rapids are runnable but quite challenging. The power is definitely there, making for challenging sequences of whitewater for advanced kayakers.

99\.9 is starting to be pretty high. The boogie sections are excellent and even starting to be quite exciting in some parts! However, some of the big drops will definitely be very stacked sequences.

At 100.0 and over, you are venturing into a continuous and big river. At this point, most rapids are either class V+ sequences in the middle of nowhere or completely unrunnable. If you run the Fildegrand at these water levels, you are there to enjoy the boogie with a few portages.

The river: The river starts with a short flat, leading into four mile rapid. This is a 2 or 3km rapid at high water, and a few class II-III rapids separated by pools at low water. After a few rapids, you will get into one that is a little bit longer and steeper. This rapid then drops off into an S bend. Start on the left and go right then back to the left. Square up with holes and lean into curlers. This turns into more class III boogie which leads into a series of lakes. This lake ends into 2 boogie rapids before the Killer B’s. This is a series of drops that is usually the biggest challenge of the day. The first drop is an 8ft waterfall landing onto a flat shelf into a retentive hole. Rope safety is crucial below it as the rest of the rapid is right downstream. Scout and portage on river right. The next one is a set of holes and reactionaries leading immediately into a 50ft cascading slide. Scout and portage on the left. A few kilometers of flat follow until dixon dam, a simple class III rapid. After this, the boogie become continuous and high quality. Eventually, it winds down and around a left hand band comes Devil’s chute. This rapid is a wide chute ending into a big hole at the bottom. There is a fun middle line at lower flows consisting of catching an eddy halfway down and ferrying into the middle into a big boof. At high flows, the hole becomes too big, and this rapid is mostly run along the right shore. Scout and portage on river right. Some boogie follows before another horizon line. This is a small slide called child’s play. It looks like a large drop while it isn’t. However, at higher flows the hole at the bottoms gets quite large. Run on the right. This gets you to striations. This is a small rock canyon where all the water constricts. At 99.8, it is a challenging sequence with a sticky hole at the top. At 99.7, it is a sweet little canyon rapid. At high water, the holes become huge and a sneak channel opens up on river left. This channel has a few drops and ends into an 8ft boof. The next rapid is immediately downstream. The left channel is where most of the water goes and is called untouchables. It is a big vertical drop landing on shelf that goes out onto a big long slide. This has been run but is walked by many. Walk on the rocks river right and run the side channel. At higher water, the top of the right channel goes. It is a nice curtain waterfall on the left with a pin rock in the center. The rest is class III boogie with a few class IV rapids that are mainly read and run. This tapers down to moving flatwater until the river goes out into the Dumoine.

Takeout: Option 1 — the ATV trail. There is an ATV trail that runs along side of the river in river right starting at Dixon dam. It ends at Bertrand cabin on the Dumoine tote road trail. Find on a map, and follow this trail and leave an ATV there. You should have a small trailer for kayaks or a side by side with roof racks as kayaks don’t fit width wise on four wheeler racks through the trail (we’ve tried 😔). Takeout once you see a white trapper cabin on river right.

Option 2 — paddle right out. Once you get to the Dumoine, run down another 13 or so kilometers until Ryan’s chute, the last rapid before the Ottawa. Run it or portage/scout on river left. Paddle another 6km out of the mouth and 3 to cross the Ottawa river to the Stonecliffe boat launch on Township hall Rd.

Bonus: If the river is running in late spring, summer, or early fall, give a call to Dumoine valley guides. The owner of the Business might be able to pick you up at the bottom of Ryan’s chute via a pontoon boat and bring you back to Rapides-des-Joachims. If it is within season, please support his business. It’s a total win win.

Happy paddling!

There's an additional manky drop just upstream and a nice 10 footer warm up. Downstream of these falls is Chute Caesar which are a good time. This park and huck can be combined with Gens de Terre section for a great weekend.

sections.name: Haute Gatineau
sections.description:
Classic Quebec run through the magnificent Gatineau river.\
Starting from Maniwaki down to the Bonnet Rouge camping area in Bouchette.

This river used to bring a lot of wood down to Ottawa. There is still a good amount of trunks piled up at Le Corbeau.\
The marked put-in is now only used during the Festival de la haute Gatineau, by special permission on the land owner.\
The owners of Bonnet Rouge now have another put-in upstream of Lucifer that should be leveraged for the rest of the year.\
The owners of Bonnet Rouge charge for the use of the put-in and take-out.

The following map shows the named rapids, plus festival put-in and regular take-out:\
<https://gatineau.org/the-river/#impact-riv-en>\
\
Water level is updated frequently by the people at [Bonnet Rouge](https://www.bonnetrougerafting.com/).

Flows:

* 163.22 = 14

* 163.33 = 15

* 163.85 = 21

* 164.35 = 26

River can be round throughout the season. There is a guaranteed 10cms from upstream dam. At low flows, the river is creeky and the waterfall becomes friendlier. It can also be run very high, with some rapids then requiring portaging (such as waterfall), but other rapids become big water fun!\
\
Can be done as a multiday or as a single day trip, however, if doing as a single day, do start very early and ideally camp at put in as the shuttle is approximately 1 hour long. \

sections.name: Creek
sections.description:
Ruisseau Jackson (un affluent de la Yamaska Sud-Est, dans l'estrie). Ça pend environ 2 heures à faire et c'est vraiment un beau petit creek. C'est une quinzaine de drops 3-4.\
\
C'est un beau petit creek, dans un décor de petites falaises de roche et mousse, et neige j'imagine..... Il draine le mont Écho. Il y a une section en amont, mais un peu trop extrême. Et c'est une quinzaine de drops 3-4 avec une chute à portager. Un autre kayakiste l'a fait avant nous pour la 1re fois, et sur son échelle de valeur, il lui donne un 9/10. C'est vraiment un beau creek.\
\
Le put-in: 45.1306056,-72.5128412 (Gauge - Dalle de Roche juste aval du pont)\
\
Le take-out: 45.1593874,-72.5456135\
\
La première chute est un infran; un gros bloc en barre la fin. Et attention au riverain sur la rive gauche de la chute. Il semble très frustré par les plaisanciers nombreux en été . Aussi bien ne pas zieuter la chute de près afin de ne pas attirer son attention, plutôt couper à travers, rive droite, pour reprendre à la base de la chute et après le coude. Attention à un autre infran 75m plus loin dans un goulot suivant le coude. Le court rapide entre les 2 infran est genre IV+, Sortie impérative tout de suite après rive droite pour éviter le 2nd infran. Le reste se fait bien avec des seuils nets et verticaux.\
\
Si on ignore la chute, l'autre infran et le court rapide entre les deux, c'est genre IV-. À noter qu'avec la nature resserrée du cours d'eau, la crue doit avoir un impact majeur sur la difficulté.

<https://qcrivers.blogspot.com/2007/10/ruisseau-jackson-estrie.html>

sections.name: Pont-Rouge
sections.description:
This section is likely one of the prettiest and most frequented around Quebec. With cliffs on most of its length, the section isolates the paddlers.

At lower levels, beginners will enjoy the multiple small surfing waves. (50-120).\
From 150-200 cumecs, the sections becomes more difficult and fun for more experienced paddlers, with many playspots.\
From 400-800 cumecs, this becomes one of the best runs in Quebec with difficult recoveries now.\
Above 800, the Râpe à Fromage now sports waves up to 5m and only advanced paddlers should attempt this section at those levels.

The narrow canyon starting at the Chemin du Roy and ending at the power station is often skipped for the easier start in front of the power station.

At the start, leave the car at the parking lot located on the South side of the Chemin du Roy and Du Collège corner.\
The parking area at the take-out is at the Parc des berges.

sections.name: Taureau
sections.description:
This section requires a long shuttle. A bunny shuttle is always the easier way, in addition only one will have to pay the entry fees to the national park. The put in is located in the "secteur des jumeaux" on the 175 going North. The take out is on the 33km of chemin du parc-nationnal.

It is not necessary to have a 4x4 truck but you will be happy to have one because the road can be ruff.

The section is 25 km long and 15 km of class IV-V. Extra equipment like a break-down paddle is very much needed.

**Water levels**

The ponderation is about 1/3 so for example if the J-C is at 80 cms the section will be around 25 cms

sections.name: Tewkesbury
sections.description:
Just a short drive from Quebec city, this is one of the most popular sections of the Jaques Cartier. It is short enough to get a couple of runs in a day with out rushing, but has plenty of variety. Several companies run commercial rafting trips down here, but it is by no means crowded.

The river level responds to rain quite rapidly, and the online gauge is downstream of the run. A good check is to note the reading on the painted gauge, on the downstream side of the bridge at the put in. -1.5 on this gauge is about 30 cumecs, and +1 is roughly 100 cumecs. It is runnable higher, it just gets bigger and faster!

\+3.5 = 180 m3 : IV+/ V \
\+3.0 = 160 m3 : IV+ \
\+2.0 = 125 m3 : IV+ Niveau Haut \
\+1.0 = 95 m3 : IV/IV+ \
\+0.5 = 75 m3 : IV/IV+ Max. pour intermédiaire \
\+0.0 = 60 m3 : IV Niveau moyen \
\-1.0 = 40 m3 : IV Ideal pour boof\
\-2.0 = 20 m3 : IV Niveau Bas

The rapids start off easy enough, and have calm bits between them that are long enough for comfort, but keep the interest coming. Soon, a long rapid called the Meat Grinder (Hache Viande) is reached. This is more difficult than anything so far, and has a ledge on the left at the bottom. After a few more fun rapids, a longer pool is reached, above a rapid on a left hand bend. Stay to the inside of the corner, as there is a wide sticky pour-over that extends from the right bank to the centre of the river. Shortly after is Surprise rapid, with a runnable drop on the left, and some fun technical lines centre and right.

This is the last of the difficult rapids, and soon the river splits, the left being the more usual route. Easy (and sometimes shallow) rapids continue from here to just above the take out. The take out is on the left.

**To get to the take out;** Get on QC-175 North out of Quebec city, and take exit 167 (Stoneham/Tewkesbury). Follow this for about 15 km until the river can be seen to the right. A track leads down to the river. This is a rafting take out, so may be locked or busy. If in any doubt, continue downstream and ask at Nouvelle Vague Rafting.

**To get to the put in;** Go back the way you came, and in Tewkesbury, turn left onto Rue Jaques Cartier S. You will pass a bridge over the river on your left, and park on the right shortly afterwards. There is a launching spot just opposite the parking.

<http://www.canadariversguidebook.ca/section/jaques-cartier/tewkesbury-section/>

sections.name: Main
sections.description:
* **Grade:** 3+(5)P

* **Level:** Needs about 25 cumecs for a good run down. The higher it is, the harder it gets!

* **Gauge:** [Online](http://www.cehq.gouv.qc.ca/suivihydro/graphique.asp?NoStation=042610)

* **Length:** 15\.5 km

* **Time:** 3-5 hours

* **Put In:** Below the Laniel Dam

* **Take Out:** Confluence with the Ottawa

* **Shuttle:** 19 km

* **Character:** Classic intermediate run

The Kipawa is one of the Canadian classics, only marred by how far North it is, and its status as a threatened river. The annual [Kipawa Rally](http://www.kipawariver.ca/index_en.html) is a good time to go and share the good times on the river, and offers a water release most years. There is something for everyone here, from relatively sedate river runners, to the new school playboaters, and the waterfall hucksters. The take out is along a dirt road that might not be passable in early season.

Get on river left below the dam. This used to be runnable, but alas no more. Never fear though, there is plenty of fun to be had downstream, with all of the rapids having something to entertain you. If it is your first time down, look out for a right hand bend where the river begins to steepen up, and make sure you get out on the right bank, as the Grande Chute is downstream!

For almost all water levels this is a definite portage, and even in low water it is a formidable piece of water. Have a look from the viewing platform and decide for yourself. Get on at the base, and paddle down another rapid to the top of Hollywood, the last rapid. This has the Pete's Dragon drop part way down, again, only really runnable in low water. Portage on the right once more, and hold on to your hat for the fun bouncy ride down to the take out. There is a fairly large stopper somewhere down on the right to avoid. Once you hit the lake at the bottom, get out on the right bank. This is private property, so be respectful please.

**To get to the take out;** From Temiscaming, drive North on QC-101 for 50 km to Laniel. The road goes over the dam, and there is a path down on river left.

**To get to the put in;** Continue North for 10 km, and turn left onto Chemin Grande Chute. After 7 km, turn right onto a private road. This goes to the private residence at the take out. The gate may be locked, so be prepared to park on the side of the road and walk the rest. Continuing on Chemin Grande Chute goes to a parking lot and short walking trail to the Grande Chute viewpoint.

**Water level**

Needs about 25 cumecs for a good run down. The higher it is, the harder it gets!

*This text comes from the following [source](http://www.canadariversguidebook.ca/section/kipawa/) under the CC license*

*Maximal level is like more than 200 mc3, prime 100 mc3, low 30 mc3. Festival level it around 140mc3. Mathieu Caron, 2024*

sections.name: Express
sections.description:
Clean express creek for advanced paddler. Almost every rapids is scoutable form the road. The run starts with a class 2 rapids followed by a short flat section. After that you really need to scope or take out before the three drop section. This section of class V rapids are stout and needs to be really looked into before sending them. The last one is a big slide that end in a pool but create a big sticky hole at higher flows.

You then cross a small pool that leads you into a short class II-III section that ends into the next big rapids a class IV+ cascade with a clean center line. If you want to stop before or walk the rapid there is a big eddy on the left side of the river. This rapids is in three part. First a drop into a rover wide hole then a smaller drop into another hole followed by a clean vein on the extreme right of the river be on the lookout for trees at higher level.

You then enter a class 2 section that ends on a flat section.

Following this flat is the last section which is made of class III rapids with a big section of holes that are sometime sticky depending on the level.

YOU NEED TO STOP RIGHT BEFORE THE BRIDGE AT HIGHER FLOWS. USUALLY WATER GOES OVER THE BRIDGE WHICH CREATE A DANGEROUS SYPHON.

You take out on the left behind the cabins or directly on the pourvoirie coin lavigne depending if you can pass under the bridge or not.

///

Belle section express pour pagayeurs avancés. Presque tout les rapides sont observable depuis la route de terre qui longe la rivière. Il est recommandé d'aller observer les rapides avant de faire cette section.

La rivière commence par un court R2 de réchauffement suivi d'une courte section d'eau calme.

Suite à cela, vous entrez directement dans la section classe 5 de la descente qui est constitué de trois seuils/chutes imposante. Une fois engagé il est impossible de sortir de cet enchainement. Cette section se termine par une grosse slide qui finie dans un bassin où un gros trou collant se crée à plus haut niveau.

À la fin de ce bassin commence une courte section de classe 2-3 qui débouche sur une série de cascade de classe 4-4+ selon les niveaux. Il est important de s'arrêter rive gauche histoire de bien évaluer sa ligne et/ou de marcher la première cascade rive gauche.

La fin de cette secrion de cascades nous amène à une petite section classe 2 qui fini dans un grand bassin. Suivi de la dernière section de rapide constituée d'un train de vague classe 3 avec plusieurs grosse vagues/trou qui pourraient surprendre un débutant.

ATTENTION SI VOUS FAITE LA SECTION À PLUS HAUT NIVEAU À SORTIR AVANT LE PONT DE LA RIVIÈRE L'ASSOMPTION QUI, À EAUX HAUTES, EST SOUS L'EAU ET CONSTITUE UNE SIVE DANGEREUSE.

On peut donc sortir du coté gauche de la rivière derrière les chalets locatifs ou si le niveau le permet au stationnement pourvoirie coin lavigne.

sections.name: Allouez dam to bridge
sections.description:
Sweet little class II-IV section from lac Allouez to the bridge on the Maganasipi river. Take the trail to the dam and leave a vehicle at the kiosk near the bridge (putin for the canyon section). Starts of with swamps slowly creeping it’s way into a sick gorge. Soon enough, the river starts to pick up gradient showing itself in long shallow rapids and pleasant little sliding drops and little boofs. The rapid sections are separated by meandering swamps filled with wildlife. Be careful if you run this in the fall as it is prime moose hunting territory. I would recommend wearing an orange vest during these times. Also, you will encounter a few strainers in between rapids until you get to one specific gorge. A huge log jam resumes itself into a 5ft wide slot where there isn’t much gradient but there’s lots of branches from a fallen tree dangling into the riverbed making the rapid a bad call. Get out and walk on river left. This is towards the end of the run. Don’t forget to pay your road passes for Zecs Rapides-des-Joachims, Dumoine and Maganasipi.

Happy paddling!

sections.name: Canyon
sections.description:
To get to this section, you must make sure you pay the fee to enter the Zec Dumoine and the Zec Maganasipi. They are both minimal. Drive up to the put in and make sure you arrange the logistics far before you leave for your trip as they can get complicated. You can camp at the put in as this section, while short, may require an entire day. Put in the next morning and start the journey down the canyon!

You will immediately see the geology of the river change as it engorges itself in epic rock formations and tall engorged banks. Some boogie water leads into the first drop. At low water, it is preferable to wlak the entrance and run the meat at the bottom. There is also a boof line on river left that maybe possible. A few hundred meters later, things get sporty with a 70ft slide. The entrance is a 8ft, pretty chill slide leading straight into the meat. There is a benign curler towards the bottom that shoots you into a steep chute with a fairly sharp rock skip at the bottom. However, even tho it may look manky, it is not that bad even as low flows. A few small rapids after, the river drops significantly. Another 60ft steep slide lies ahead. In low water, it is preferable to portage as there are a bunch of nasty flakes everywhere. There is one possible line on river right forcing you to enter backwards in order to go down frontwards! Another pleasant 12ft drop lies below as you make your way through a truly amazing rock gorge. Once the boulder garden stops, there is unfortunately a mandatory portage as the whole river goes under a HUGE rock pile. It is possible to walk right on top of it. Following the big portage, there are a few other small rapids out into lake Macon where the section ends. You then have to get on on river left and hike back to the road to set shuttle.

Happy paddling!

sections.name: Lac Macon to Ottawa river
sections.description:
If you’ve done the canyon section, you may seriously want to consider dunning the whole thing out, at least to the alternate takeout at the bridge a few km before the mouth. From that bridge on, the river is roadside until a bunch of switchbacks. You really only miss a sweet class III-IV drop and a whole bunch of class II boogie in a sick gorge. Once you’re out of lac Macon, there’s a big drop that must be scouted with caution. Run river middle right or walk river right. After that, you engage yourself in an epic canyon with some boogie and a few class III boofs and long rapids. Some class I-II boogie for kilometers follows out to lac Johnson. From there, paddle a few k of flat to get to Johnson dam which is just a class II rapid. A few more rapids and flat pools later and you get to the bridge. Don’t forget to pay your road passes for the following zecs: Rapides-des-Joachims, Dumoine, and most importantly Maganasipi

Side note: I strongly encourage wearing an orange vest during hunting season. Same goes for the 2 other sections upstream.

Happy Paddling!

sections.name: Ouest Magpie
sections.description:
This trip starts with a small extremely low water creek accessible by a train. Another option to avoid the creek is to fly in.

**Maps used:** 1:250000 (22P, 22I) 1:50000 (22P/13, 22P/14, 22P/11, 22P/6 22P/3, 22P/2, 22I/15, 22I/8, 22I/9, 22I/10)\
**General weather:** average for September. We did not get snow at the put in, or in the first few days. But we have heard of people getting it. We did have ice in our bottles one morning.\
**Pace of trip:** relaxed\
**Campsites:** The flat water approach has many beaches and lots of cozy pockets in the woods, great camping. The West Magpie has lots of great camping as well. Lac Magpie is absolutely stunning and has plenty of beaches. We found that the Magpie was not as nice camping.\
**Whitewater overview:** The West Magpie is steep, continuous mountain river, a lot can go wrong out there. Scout often! The West Magpie has also been known to flash flood with sustained large amounts of rain. The Magpie has a more traditional big water feel.\
**Sept Iles:** Great Town to Start an expedition in. It has everything that you would need, Walmart, Tims Ext. Hot Tip, Gino's has really good pizza! To sleep; there is a wide variety of hotels and motels, a thriving Tinder scene, the Walmart parking lot, and a quiet little park near the train station.\
**The Train:** Take it!!! it is so beautiful out there, never once was our group board. The train ride in is absolutely stunning. The “flat” water is not really that bad as the river moves at around 2km/hr and has occasional rapids. It also gives you time to figure out your systems for your group; morning rituals, packing your boat and how your boat feels fully loaded. Lastly: YOU HAVE TO EARN YOUR TURNS!!! nuff said. The Train ticket is fixed, but your kayak can cost really what ever the person at the desk decides to charge you. Do not call it a kayak, do not call it a canoe. Say its a wave ski and its over 4ft long. Hopefully this will cost less then a canoe (140$)\

sections.name: Mauvaise
sections.description:
*\*\*\* Cette rivière a besoin de 1 journée de 30-40mm de pluie au nord-ouest de St-Raymond. Mais il faut qu'il pleuve au bon endroit car la Mauvaise prend sa source des ruisseaux de son petit bassin versant. Le meilleur moyens d'être sûr du niveau est d'aller voir l'échelle au take-out. Si elle ne roule pas parce qu'il n'a pas assez plu, la Tourilli peut être une option viable sinon Neilson pour les pagayeurs de classe V.*

Échelle située en aval à droite du pont au take-out.

Les deux jumeaux se passent très bien au centre.

Ensuite, avant la slide, il y a un R4 qui se passe bien à gauche pour ensuite faire un arrêt avant de descendre la chute.

Ensuite, nous arrivons à la bosse qui est une double drop d'environ 2 mètres. Elle se fait bien de la droite jusqu'au 3/4 de la rivière. Évitez d'aller extrême gauche.

Ensuite arrive la dalle qui est une suite de glissade. En haut de 12m/3, il se forme un trou au centre.

Ensuite une belle section de R2-3 avant d'arriver au Suisse. Ce rapide se passe bien partout. Ensuite la rivière se sépare en deux. À droite, c'est serré; coller la gauche pour ensuite aller à la droite au centre du rapide. À la gauche, se trouve le trou, un bon coup de pagaie avec de la vitesse vous fera passer par dessus sans problème.

Ensuite, se trouve le petit punch (R4) allez chercher la déferlante à droite pour prendre de la vitesse et défoncer le porte-feuille. Ensuite arrive Nagasaki qui est une copie du Suisse; plusieurs arrêts sont possibles. S'en suit d'une série de R2-3 et E.V. Pour finir, arrive le dernier rapide avant le pont, la Quille(R3). Tout de suite après le pont, le take-out est à gauche. Si la rivière est en bas de -2 sur l'échelle la descente est déconseillée.

**Water levels**

*Manual gauge:*

min 7 CMS

opt 12 CMS

max 25 CMS

sections.name: Trou de la Fée
sections.description:
Une des rivières les plus populaires de la région du Saguenay-Lac-Saint-Jean, la basse Métabetchouane a changé de visage depuis les fameuses crues démentielles de l'été 1996.

La descente est praticable pendant presque toute la saison. Les difficultés augmentent avec le débit. Une échelle graduée en face de la mise-à-l'eau nous indique le niveau de la section. Selon les pagayeurs locaux, la section est intéressante à partir de -2 et à son meilleur autour de 0. La station hydrométrique 061502, localisée 1,8 km en amont nous indique le débit.

La section se termine avec le Cimetière, un rapide rocailleux, annonçant la fin du parcours et la traversée du lac jusqu'à la sortie utilisée par la compagnie de rafting H2O.

[Mis-a-l'eau alternative](https://maps.app.goo.gl/RDNFxUCaHkhbfaJx7)  (pour eviter le parc si c'est ferme)

On peut mettre à l'eau environ 20-30m après la chute. (la marche est de 300m environ)\
\
Sortie d'eau: au pont du chemin Mountain 5km plus au sud sur la 245 puis a gauche sur la 243 et presque tout de suite a gauche sur le chemin Mountain. On fera la sortie rive gauche après le pont (les riverains sont d'accord si on leur demande d'abord la permission). Quelques autos peuvent être stationnées après le pont coté droit mais la place est limitée en largeur car on empiète un peu sur le chemin.

sections.name: C
sections.description:
On the edge of civilization is what this river feels like. Just out of the small hamlet of Saint-Stanislas, the Mistassibi is a paddlers delight. This river feels big, with wide, powerful rapids that are fairly continuous.

The first rapids are short, but a good warm up for what is to come. Mid-way through the run is a long, complex rapid that is about 1 km in length. Many routes are possible. After a flatter section, the largest two rapids, Hawaii, and Black Mass (Messe Noire) are in quick succession. Have a look at them if you haven't run them before. After that, easier water leads to the take out on the outside of a sweeping left bend. In the spring time there is an enormous playwave that forms at Black Mass.

**To get to the take out;** From Dolbeau-Mistassini, take QC-169S, and turn left onto Rang St Louis. When you get to Saint-Stanislas, go and check the current access situation with Wally and his crew at the excellent [Quebec Raft](https://www.quebecraft.com/) at the Western end of town. The usual access has been along a dirt road at the end of Avenue Hebert.

Check out the alternative raft take out on the "POI" tab in the whitewater.guide app

**To get to the put in;** From Saint-Stanislas, take Chemin de l'Eden for 5km, then turn right onto Chemin du lac Eden. The put in is on the right once you see the river.

<http://www.canadariversguidebook.ca/section/mistassibi/>

sections.name: Lac Ashuanipi Crossover
sections.description:
This is the most physical option. We chose this option because we could not enter Labrador during Covid (I mean we did, just not any cities). This route is great for the following people; those looking for adventure, those that have done the Pekans, and parties less than three. although it is the hardest physically it is the easiest logistically. take the train from Sept iles to station Orway, and off you go. \
[Annotated map for the Ashuanipi crossover approach](http://www.cartespleinair.org/Canot/07/AshuanipiMoisieGoutier2019.pdf)

Below is the trip report with the coordinates in UTM

**Number of days:** 15\
**Water Level:** Low at the top medium high at the bottom.\
**Trip Dates:** Two weeks at the end of September into the start of october \
**Total KM:** 410\
**Total Elevation drop:** 1750ft\
**General weather:** Average for end of September and early October. We had two nights well below zero, and some flurries. consistent rain on the river, which is normal for this river (the name Moisie is French for moldy). We were very lucky on the lakes and only got wind bound for half a day.\
**Pace of trip:** Pushed at the start through the lakes, relaxed on the river.\
**Campsites:** Mostly bush crash sites throughout the trip, we could always find a cozy mossy den. Lots of burn on the river so it made camping a little easier. We counted only 5 actual established sites on the river, most of which were in a poor state.\
\
**Lake Overview:** Ashuanipi is a huge lake! It can get very big and windy very fast. We were very lucky we had dead calm and were able to take a big chunk out of it the first day. We planned a half day of being windbound. We could have been wind bound a full day, and could have made the time up on the River. That being said being windbound and slowed more than a couple of days it would start to get dicey. \
**Crossover Overview:** the portage is easy to find and follow, at the time of this writing it was very well flagged (thank you, thank you). It is wet and physically challenging. The creak is a drag (literally)\
**Whitewater overview:** This is probably one of the best class 3/4 multiday rivers in the world. Overall you want low at the top, high at the bottom. There are a variety of different styles of white water from creaky side channels to supper fun big water rapids. We had low water up at the top, and could have had a wee bit more (half a foot to a foot)**,** and medium high at the bottom. The last days rapids were an incredible 650cms of huge waves, some pushing 12ft tall. It reminded us of high water Lunch Stop waves on the Ottawa River, for 6km. The Moisie floods easily, but can take water. During our last week we saw the river rise about 2ft.\
 \
The upper section has a creaky vibe and has 3 class 5 rapids, the rest are no harder than 4+, but mostly class 2/3. The River changes to big water after the Pekan river confluence. From here down there are 4 class 5 rapids, all are easily portaged or creaked around. A note on Rapid Katchapahun. It needs very, very low water to be runnable, any amount of rain will likely boost it to an unrunable flow. The portage is a workout, but you have to earn your turns.\
**Schedule:** We planned on going for 16 days (plus an extra day of food) we cut it short because it was raining during the good hiking days. we would highly recommend bringing the same amount of food so you can hike. We hiked a lot in 2011 and it was incredible.\
**Sept Iles:** Great Town to Start an expedition in. It has everything that you would need, Walmart, Tims Ext. Hot Tip, Gino's has really good pizza, but Marcos in Baie Comeau is better. To sleep; there is a wide variety of hotels and motels, a thriving Tinder scene, the Walmart parking lot, and a quiet little park near the train station. \
**The Train:** Take it!!! it is so beautiful out there, never once was our group board. The train ride is absolutely stunning. The flat water is not really that bad (although it can get quite windy), and It also gives you time to figure out your systems for your group; morning rituals, packing your boat and how your boat feels fully loaded. Lastly: YOU HAVE TO EARN YOUR TURNS!!! nuff said.\
You take the train from Sept Iles to station Orway (7hrs) and is a fixed price of 50$, but your kayak can cost really what ever the person at the desk decides to charge you. Do not call it a kayak, do not call it a canoe. Say it's a wave ski and it's over 4ft long. Hopefully this will cost less than a canoe (140$). We however payed 150 for the kayak, and 10$ extra weight. \
\
**Trip Report**\
\
**Day 0**\
HoW: 540-720pm\
Distance: 5km\
Weather: clear, light headwinds (from West), Frigid \
Campsite: 997 267 23 A/12 west (island bush crash across from station)\
\
**Day 1**\
HoW: 9-4\
Distance: 25km \
Weather: Dead calm, cool\
Campsite: 850 250 23 B/9 (esker island, burn)\
\
**Day 2**\
HoW: 10-1245\
Distance: 7km\
Weather: Heinous headwinds (from west), cold\
Campsite: 805 267 (the point)\
\
**Day 3**\
HoW: 9-530\
Distance: 16km as the crow flys\
Weather: Annoying side wind (from west), cool and clear\
Campsite: 668 288 23 B/10 (shitty bush crash in from beach)\
Tech

1. Portage 1.2 km 747 320 23 B/10. it took us about 1.5hrs Starts right beside the massive glacial erratic. There is flagger if you can't see the boulder. The trail starts directly back from the boulder. It is difficult to find and not well flagged (although the rest of the trail is well flagged). It is a 400m game trail to the first swamp (the southern tip of Lac Lune 745 320). It is then a lovely swamp walk to the creek. We portaged in the middle of Lac Lune (it is split by a passable swamp) to the forest at 743 322. There is good camping in the adjoining forest at 744 319. The trail bears west across the swamps and is well flagged. When you think you have had enough you are almost done. The end was flagged with blue flagger

2. The Creek is about as wide as a kayak and around 2km long (as the crow flies). It is long, windie and very shallow, expect to drag numerous times. It took us just over an hour to do the creek. The creek from Lac du Petit Portage is also a drag. Every narrows will have a shitty swift rock pile to drag down.

\

sections.name: Pekans-Moisie
sections.description:
The Pekans is the largest river right tributary of the moisie (and possible its largest). You access it from QC route 389 from Baie Comeau to Labrador City. It starts around 2000ft, joining the moisie around 1400ft. It is roughly 100km long and contains a variety of class 2-5 whitewater. The confluence falls are stunning, but a 1500m portage. Logistically this makes more sense with more people, as one could drive to the put in, then put the car on a cargo train to Sept Iles (roughly 450-500$). Below three people it becomes more expensive.\
[Map 1](http://www.cartespleinair.org/Canot/07/PekansMoisieGoutier2019.pdf). [Map 2](http://www.cartespleinair.org/Canot/07/PekansMoisieKovac.pdf)

The full trip report is on the [Fraser Cepella website](https://frasercepella.weebly.com/moisie-river.html?fbclid=IwAR1iv-Kd1wO3ouOB9RZLX0QyEV7V6mtsVPhxSZSMX8_vaKsrE9wsIYIIlJY).

sections.name: Traditional Lake Approach
sections.description:
Take the train from Sept Iles to the Lab city station (the train no longer runs to lab city, you are put off on the side of the highway) from there, organise a taxi (ideally pre arranged) to pick up you and your equipment and drop it off at Lac deux Miles. These lakes are less big than Ashuanipi, but are still very large. This option takes roughly the same time as the Ashuanipi crossover\
[Annotated map for the traditional lake approach](http://www.cartespleinair.org/Canot/07/DeMilleMoisieGoutier2019.pdf)

The full trip report is on the [Fraser Cepella website](https://frasercepella.weebly.com/moisie-river.html?fbclid=IwAR1iv-Kd1wO3ouOB9RZLX0QyEV7V6mtsVPhxSZSMX8_vaKsrE9wsIYIIlJY).

sections.name: Guy’s lap
sections.description: Wicked creeking with a waterfall that all the old people around here tell me isn’t runnable. Class II - III above and below the falls which are 2 km North of the highway. There is also a sweet put-in at the Falls which is just West of the government salt yard. Do not follow the road all the way to the end, take a trail on the left just before you go downhill. Can be run to the sea or to a wonderful take out called Vieux Moulin but is class II after the highway 11 bridge. The creek has a tendency to be low. If the line of shark's tooth rocks in the lowest pool of the falls are visible she's a no go. Named Guy’s Lap after a kayaking legend in the area.

sections.name: Du Village
sections.description:
Beautiful beginner section. The put-in and take out are easy to find and the river is a fun and semi continous wave train perfect for an afterwork lap or to initiate beginners to eddy catching.

Mid way trough the run you will face a smal cascade (III) you can walk it river left or run it on the left side. Pretty easy but usually pretty shallow so be aware of rocks.

Under the last bridge right before the take out at higher flows there is a fun wave to surf there.

Take no more than 2h to do the  run.
///

Belle section pour débutant ou pour une petite balade après le travail. La rivière est belle et est constituée d'un train de vague semi-continue sur 3km.

À la moitié de la descente il y a une courte cascade (III) qui se portage sur la gauche. Si vous la faite, la ligne centre gauche est la plus évidente. Cette cascade est peu profonde alors attention aux roches!

La section se termine sous un pont/barrage qui, à des niveaux plus élevés, offre une belle petite vague à surf.

La section prend au maximum 2h à faire.

sections.name: Mountain Chutes
sections.description:
A very fun steep slide with roadside access.

Class 2 before the drop with a couple smaller rapids before meeting the road again. Located close to the Ottawa Valley. Combined with running other drops on the near coulonge it is a very worthwhile day trip.

sections.name: Britannia to the ruins
sections.description:
A fun little section. Great for after work laps.
You should be able to do class 3s without problems in order to do this run safely.

Note this section does not go through the ruins ( old abandoned Stone collapsing buildings)...you will see tgese larger rapids down river from the put in parking (parking du Parc des  rapides-deschenes). The Rapids running through the ruins are is much higher class with dangerous metal rebar. You should not be going through any stone buildings on this run at all. They are extremely dangerous.

Park at :

Parking du Parc des Rapides-Deschênes
https://maps.app.goo.gl/PL3RCw5HNg8reqHj8

Walk up river 400m or so, ferry to the Ontario side a little more than halfway.  You are closer to the Ontario side. This is about 600 meters. The river is quite wide here and may be quite intimidating but the run is easy. Follow the main flow the best you can. Going far river right and you'll end up in a rock section.  Too far river left and you'll be in some ledges, both of these runs will be easier than the main run.

This run is what Wilderness Tours rafting uses as their easier runs. Easier than the Ottawa main and middle routes that they offer.

After the run, you can play in the eddys and holes on your way back to the Qc. Side.

You will find a play wave behind the ruins, almost as far river left as you can get. Find the eddy behind the last stone structure and ferry into the wave.

Take out at the bottom of the ruins on the Qc. side and walk back up the bike path bordering the river.

Note: Stay away from the ruins! You should not be going through or into any of them on this run.

Happy paddling: Mayo

sections.name: Dessert wave
sections.description:
Dessert wave located in the middle of the Ottawa River. Not necessarily for the faint of heart . You must have a bomb proof roll as the swim is not an option. However if you do swim the island down stream is your best location. If you get pushed past that, ditch your boat swim to shore as the Chaudier dam isn't too far now.
The wave is smooth and the wave train after is also smooth so a roll isn't bad. You should be able to roll in class 3 and 4 white water to be taking this run.

Put in is the same spot as the take out. The end of this section is shown as the middle of the river where the actual wave is. Paddle upriver directly toward Bates Island 200m then work your way to the Ontario side another 100m or so. You'll see the large white cap of Desert wave. Desert wave is the largest and fist wave of the bunch and usually the only one surfable.  Once you fall off work your way to the Quebec side and walk your way back up the the takeout/putin and repeat. Laps take 20 to 25 minutes plus the surf time.

Surfers and paddle boarders can walk up the  shelf in low water about 0ft on the river guage. Not recommended for kayakers as you can't get into your boat quick enough after. There are a few other shelves that give you eddy help on your way back. You can even walk up the island near the Quebec show to save a little time.
