sections.name: Main
sections.description:
New gauges available from Hydro-Quebec, need verification / De nouvelles jauges sont disponibles auprès d'Hydro-Québec, mais elles doivent être vérifiées.

* **Grade:** 3-4 (5 high water or above 11 on OWL/shaggy gauge)

* **Level:** Any! Can be paddled anytime it is free of ice (Main from Lorne down is generally ice free all year round but only recommended for experts in these extreme conditions)

* **Gauge:** The physical gauge is below the first rapid (McCoys chute), and is the one that all other sources reference. There is an [online gauge](http://shaggydesigns.com/gauge/ottawa.htm) now, maintained by [Shaggy Designs](http://shaggydesigns.com/index.htm). Several other websites report the readings, but may not update very regularly: [OWL Rafting (Twitter)](https://twitter.com/OTTRiverLevel), or ask on the [Boatwerks message board](http://www.boatwerks.net/boater-board/1).

* **Length:** 9 km

* **Gradient:** 2.8 m/km

* **Time:** The main is fast! at high flows, laps from Lorne to take out can take as little as 15 minutes.

* **Put In:** The OWL Public put in off McCoy Chute trail or Lorne put in to skip some flat but also McCoys.

* **Take Out:** RiverRun rafting base (access fee)

* **Shuttle:** 9 km

* **Character:** Pool-drop at low flows, continuous big waves at mid to high flows. Note that death ledge comes in above 11 and has killed before. The main becomes basically continuous at high flows and scouting/catching eddies becomes hard. Home to some giant wave surfing and classics such as buseater family (big bus, mini bus, short bus), gladiator, garburator, waikiki, chopping block, etc.

Probably the best and certainly the most popular boating destination in Eastern Canada. The Ottawa has something for everyone. There are eddylines and waves small enough for beginners, big ones for the hotshots to practice their wonkyflops, and some seriously large rapids in the springtime. The section is just short enough for two runs in a day. The access is good for park 'n' play and there are usually other boaters around to hook up shuttles, cheer you on or pick up the pieces below the rapids.

The river is deceptively flat from the takeout, but follow the roaring sound (and the hordes of kayakers and rafts) towards the first rapid, McCoys Chute. McCoys can be a float down a simple wavetrain or the thundering nemesis of many a beginner boater. It all depends on water level. Inspect or portage on the right. The waves that form in this rapid (Corner wave, Horseshoe hole, Babyface) offer some great playboating. If you are new here, just follow the crowds to figure out what is best. Keep an eye upstream for boaters and rafts bearing down on you - they can't stop!

Once you have tired yourself out spinning and blunting at McCoys, head downstream, past the outflow of the Sullivan dam on your left. The gauge should be visible in the middle distance. Going straight on will take you down the [Middle channel](http://www.canadariversguidebook.ca/section/ottawa/rocher-fendu-middle-channel/). To get to the Lorne (the next rapid), take a right turn either before or after the gauge.

\
___________\
NOTE: depending on your skills and water levels, choose which channel is appropriate for you and your group!\
Above 5 on the gauge, the main becomes more challenging and is more suited for intermediates or those who have a roll but are still learning. **ABOVE 10, THE MAIN BECOMES CONTINUOUS, BOILY, AND PUSHY AND SHOULD ONLY BE ATTEMPTED BY SOLID BOATERS WITH BOMBPROOF ROLLS/STRONG FERRY SKILLS AS DEATH LEDGE LURKS BELOW!!** If the river is above 10 and you do not have a bombproof roll + strong ferry abilities, do not attempt the main. Catching the eddy between Normans and Coliseum can become hard and running Coli blind/without knowing where DEATH LEDGE is, could become very dangerous and even deadly as the name implies.

\
\
Both routes to the main converge around some islands and in a short while you will see the top of the Lorne. This is the home of the famous Garburator wave in low water (1 to -1), and Buseater in high water (15 to 16 for best levels). There are trails on both banks, so you can run it again and again. There is public access here, courtesy of OWL rafting and Whitewater Ontario.

Just below the Lorne, there are a few shelters on the right where rafting clients get lunch. Opposite these the river splits around some rocky islands - only visible in low water. If there is a queue of boaters in an eddy then Pushbutton is in! This is a great wave for practicing new moves without the nasal enema risk of a wipeout on the Garburator. Follow the current down to Butcher's Knife. This rapid gets its name from the sharp rocks on the right shore, but all difficulties can easily be avoided by running left. At higher flows, you can find the famous whirpools known to swallow kayaks for a few seconds.

Easy flowing water then leads down to Normans, a bouncy wavetrain ending in some surgy boils. This is quickly followed by Coliseum, which can be the most intimidating rapid on the river in higher flows. Again, trails are on both banks. There is an observation deck overlooking the rapid on the left - a great place to hang out and watch some raft carnage! At the bottom of Coliseum is a rocky ledge extending about halfway across the river. If this has water flowing over it, it is not a happy place to be - swim right! At flows above 10, death ledge starts to come in, making this rapid a hell of a lot more consequential. If you don't have a bombproof roll or strong ferry skills, please do not kayak the main above 10.

A couple of easier rapids follow; Dogsleg and Blacks. As is the case elsewhere on the Ottawa, there are some great playspots here in the right water levels. The river widens out at this point - the right hand channel leads past Blacks take out, and rejoins the main flow to take you down to RiverRun rafting base. This is a great spot, in a bay on river right. There is even a pub just up the hill!

In high water, there is another channel that becomes runnable; the Lost channel (purple line on the map). To access the Lost channel continue straight past the gauge, heading towards the Middle channel and turn right after about 1 km. Following the main flow in this channel to the right leads to a few pleasant drops before Anvil Falls. Anvil Falls is worth inspecting especially if you haven't seen it before. Shortly after, the main channel is rejoined above Normans.

**Take out**

Coming from Ottawa on highway 17, about 95 km from Ottawa turn right onto Storyland road. This becomes Queens line, then take a right onto Kohlsmith road (signposts for rafting). This turns into Grants Settlement road, and RiverRun rafting is on the right, or Blacks take out is just past RiverRun. Currently there is a fee to acces the take out, and only one of the take outs will be open at any given time.

**Put in**

Continue on Grants Settlement road and turn right onto McCoys Chute trail. There is a parking lot on the left, and walk down to the river.

*[Source](http://www.canadariversguidebook.ca/)* *(CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 license)*

sections.name: Middle
sections.description:
New gauges available from Hydro-Quebec, need verification / De nouvelles jauges sont disponibles auprès d'Hydro-Québec, mais elles doivent être vérifiées.

* **Grade:** 3-4(5)

* **Level:** Any! Can be paddled anytime it is free of ice

* **Gauge:** The physical gauge is below the first rapid (McCoys chute), and is the one that all other sources reference. There is an [online gauge](http://www.canadariversguidebook.ca/gauge.htm) now, maintained by [Shaggy Designs](http://www.canadariversguidebook.ca/index.htm). Several other websites report the readings, but may not update very regularly: [OWL Rafting (Twitter)](https://twitter.com/OTTRiverLevel), or ask on the [Boatwerks message board](http://www.boatwerks.net/boater-board/1)

* **Length:** 9 km

* **Gradient:** 2.8 m/km

* **Time:** Can be run top to bottom in a couple of hours, but why rush?

* **Put In:** The OWL public put in off McCoy Chute trail

* **Take Out:** RiverRun rafting base (access fee)

* **Shuttle:** 9 km

* **Character:** Pool-drop playboating heaven

Probably the best and certainly the most popular boating destination in Eastern Canada. The Ottawa has something for everyone. There are eddylines and waves small enough for beginners, big ones for the hotshots to practice their wonkyflops, and some seriously large rapids in the springtime. The section is just short enough for two runs in a day. The access is good for park 'n' play and there are usually other boaters around to hook up shuttles, cheer you on or pick up the pieces below the rapids.

The river is deceptively flat from the takeout, but follow the roaring sound (and the hordes of kayakers and rafts) towards the first rapid, McCoys Chute. McCoys can be a float down a simple wavetrain or the thundering nemesis of many a beginner boater. It all depends on water level. Inspect or portage on the right. The waves that form in this rapid (Corner wave, Horseshoe hole, Babyface) offer some great playboating. If you are new here, just follow the crowds to figure out what is best. Keep an eye upstream for boaters and rafts bearing down on you - they can't stop!

Once you have tired yourself out spinning and blunting at McCoys, head downstream, past the outflow of the Sullivan dam on your left. The gauge should be visible in the middle distance. Going right here will take you down the Main channel, but the Middle channel lies straight ahead and just left of the gauge.

About 1 km past the gauge, there is a choice of routes to take in high water. Left leads down the Magical Mystery Tour (purple line on the map), a pleasant float with some swifts that bypasses Iron Ring and S-Bend rapids. A few hundred metres straight ahead lies Iron Ring, a fairly straightforward chute in low water but a solid beatdown above 8. Inspect on the right if you are in any doubt, as Iron Ring becomes quite staunch in higher water.

If you decide that Iron Ring is not for you, then the portage is easy, or follow the channel heading right (yellow line on the map) just upstream. This channel leads to Little Trickle and Angel's Kiss rapids and bypasses Iron Ring, S-Bend and Butterfly rapids. Little Trickle has a number of routes, but most will start far left and move right for the clearest line. Angels Kiss is a fun, friendly breaking wave with a clear run-out below.

If you have paddled or portaged Iron Ring, The shallow rapid immediately downstream is aptly named S-Bend, and has a small surfwave just downstream. This is followed by Butterfly rapid, with some playboating opportunities in most waterlevels.

A small lake below Butterfly has the most impressive set of rapids at the far end. Garvin's chutes make up most of the height drop in the Middle channel, and are certainly the crux. There are 4 channels, some of which dry up in low water. They are from left to right; Staircase, S.T. chute, Dragon's Tongue and Elevator Shaft. Inspect or portage on either bank.

After Garvins, there are a number of choices of route around a few islands. Most people follow the main flow through Upper No-Name and Lower No-Name, stopping to surf whatever wave is available. Lower No-Name becomes quite big at flows around 8-12 with some scary looking holes; that being said, the best line is still down the center where it is deeper and either surf out or pull out after surfing for too long. Lower No-Name is a friendly large rapid with a big recovery pool at the bottom. The last, easy rapid is called Black Velvet and follows shortly. Again, several routes around islands are possible on the way to the take out. If it is your first time down here, stick to the main flow and keep a look out for the take outs on the right. Blacks public take out is visible as a track down to the river, while RiverRun is at the far end of a sizeable bay.

**Take out**

Coming from Ottawa on highway 17, about 95 km from Ottawa turn right onto Storyland road. This becomes Queens line, then take a right onto Kohlsmith road (signposts for rafting). This turns into Grants Settlement road, and RiverRun rafting is on the right, or Blacks take out is just past RiverRun. Currently there is a fee to acces the take out, and only one of the take outs will be open at any given time.

**Put in**

Continue on Grants Settlement road and turn right onto McCoys Chute trail. There is a parking lot on the left, and walk down to the river.

*[Source](http://www.canadariversguidebook.ca/)* *(CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 license)*

sections.description:
Aval du Lac is another classic spring run.\

<https://qcrivers.blogspot.com/2007/10/ouareau-section-aval-du-lac.html>

sections.name: Capucines
sections.description:
Classic spring run.

This sections get its name from the series of big round rocks in the main rapid.\
Preceded by Les Montagnes Russes that offer a solid ride like any good amusement park at high water.\
At mid levels, this becomes a more technical rapid with a few big holes.\
Following this, the Capucines rapid offers superb big water action at high levels and a long technical class IV rapid at mid levels.\
Center left is the general line along the whole section.\
The shuttle for this run is very long. Access to the takeout in the park is often requiring a 4x4 due melting mud and recreational vehicles.

And alternate creeking starts at the end of the dirt road Chemin de la Forêt at Domaine Appaloosa, on the Dufresne creek.\
Linking this run with the preceding Aval du Lac makes for a long and fun day with likely less shuttle efforts.

The hydrometric station is located at the top of the Darwin waterfall.\
The water in this section comes from the previous section called Aval du Lac, the Dufresne creek and the lac Blanc.

<https://qcrivers.blogspot.com/2007/10/ouareau-les-capucines.html>

sections.name: Moulin Fisk
sections.description:
Details here: https://qcrivers.blogspot.com/2007/10/combine-crabtree.html

Description Ouareau (section Moulin Fisk): Ça commence par un beau R3 d'une bonne longueur (repérage possible par la rive gauche avant même de mettre à l'eau; et aussi depuis le pont pendant la navette). Attention aux undercuts en fin de rapide! La rivière se calme ensuite et des rapides R1/EV se poursuivent dans un très beau décor avec des formations rocheuses intéressantes sur les berges. Puis le R1 se poursuit dans une partie plus large jusqu'à ce qu'on voit le pont de la 158 au loin. A partir de là, on trouve plein de beaux rouleaux qui forment un multitude de playspots (une mini Jacques-Cartier à 1H de Montréal, ça existe !!!). Après avoir bien joué, le R1 reprend (R1/2 au niveau du pont) puis un court planiol jusqu'à la sortie au niveau du quai du petit parc.

La balise hydrométrique n'étant plus très loin en aval, nous utiliserons les valeurs de celle-ci directement.

sections.name: Penniseault lake to Ottawa river
sections.description:
Description: The Penniseault creek is a fun run right out of town of the small town of the Swisha. 

Drive to Zec Rapides-des-Joachims (Swisha) zec office accross a big bridge. If the water in the Ottawa river is not too high, you will be able to see the water rushing out of the mouth of the creek if you look downstream. Drive to the very behind of the shacka nd follow the trail down to the confluence with the creek and the river to leave a vehicle there. Drive back up to the front of the shack to register yourselves. You will need to pay a small fee in order to access the zec roads. Drive up 2km and take a little trail that turns off to your right down to the first major drop. If it is high water, scout with extreme cautious and consideration. There is a smaller slide on ths way into the big one. This first rapid is no warmup so not for the faint of heart. The line ran all the time is on river left down a slide with a drop towards the bottom. Beware the sharp rocks on the outflow. The other channel is awfully close to the wall with a small undercut on the way in at low water, and an extremely ugly hole at the bottom in high flows. 

The rest is continuous class II-III+ boulder gardens with LOG JAMS TO BE INCREDIBLY CAUTIOUS ABOUT. THIS PART OF THE CREEK HAS NOT BEEN CLEARED IN A FEW YEARS SO CATCH EDDIES AND SCOUT AS MUCH AS POSSIBLE! Eventually, you will get to a rotten wooden bridge with a metal gauge near it (check the level there if you want, 10” is low runnable). To get to it from the road, take your first right up the hill and take a left. Beware of the unstable bridge tho. You can also see the horizon line of the second drop continuing straight. Back to kayaking. Keep going under that bridge and you will see 2 channels. If you wish to scout the big drop, go right and paddle accross a small secondary channel (Do not run). It is also possible to pull off park and huck laps from this location. The second chute is a funky pillow into a beefy slot that contains 2 beefy holes. The rest is a mankier and steep cascading slide into a big pourover. The last km of the run is a non stop boulder garden as it drops rapidly to the Ottawa river. 

Happy paddling!

*gabrieldeschenes266*

sections.name: Chutes de Plaisance
sections.description:
A series of waterfalls - according to google, the falls drop 206 feet in less than a kilometre.

Scout the entire set of waterfalls before even considering putting in. The put in is at the bridge, if you are intimidated by the drop under the bridge, don’t even consider running the rest.

The river left side of island at the main drop has some undercuts, scout it at low water before considering it. The river right is the main spectacle, but cleaner side. The series of falls begins with a 20 or so foot slide that transforms into a vertical 20-30 footer depending on location. Caution that the left side is shallow and has broken ankles on pitons. The center and right have their own challenges as they push you further right into the island head that is undercut. Following this drop, there’s a 20 footer directly under it, and then the canyon runout begins. The canyon has a series of 4-5 ledges which can create gnarly holes at higher water, especially the last one.

Even if you don’t kayak these, scouting them is worth it, they are very scenic! Once done, walk up the stairs and pathways back to your car.

sections.name: Papineauville
sections.description:
This section varies a great deal with levels. At spring flows (med high - high) the first rapid could be considered 4+ - 5- with a sketch spot mid way down where wood collects and must be avoided. A swirly eddy part way down can cause novice paddlers stress and a tricky ferry may be necessary. At these flows there can be a lot of fun eddies and moves to warm up for the season. Definitely class 4 (5) in the spring at high flows. Make sure to run the rapid below the big falls after the bridge. A regional highlight. Portage on the right for the big falls and take out at the Chutes de Plaisance parking lot.

The first rapid, Chutes du Portage comes up quite quickly. If you need to inspect, the right bank is OK, but you will be walking through someones garden. In most water levels, run right to avoid a stopper and strainer. The remaining wavetrain is easy. The river splits soon, the left is definitely OK, but the right is probably good as well. Things ease off for a while before another set of rapids above the imposingly named Les Portes de'l Enfer. Nothing to worry about, and there is a flat section before the take out. The bridge is really obvious, but don't miss the takeout as the Chutes de la Petite Nation are a few hundred metres downstream.

**To get to the take out;** Follow highway 50E from Gatineau about 60 km. After crossing the Petite Nation, take the next exit (signposted Plaisance), and turn left onto Montee Papineau. In 500 m, turn left at the T-intersection onto Chemin des Cascades. Another 400 m brings you to a bridge, park just after it.

**To get to the put in;** Continue on this road (now Rang Saint Louis) and in 500 m, turn right onto Rang Saint Joseph O. Follow this to the T-junction and turn right onto QC-321. After 600 m, turn right opposite no.60 into a track by some beehives, and follow for 50 m to the river. This is a fire service water supply point, so don't block access (Roadside parking on the 321 is prohibited, so beware)! To avoid the first gorge, get on below by turning into no.450 Rang Saint Joseph O and parking on the right. There is a $3/person access charge here. You can hike up from there and run the first gorge (stay respectful as its private if you go all the way up to the top).

[Source](http://www.canadariversguidebook.ca/section/petite-nation/)

<https://qcrivers.blogspot.com/2007/10/petite-nation-classique.html>

sections.name: Grande Pikauba de la 169 au lac Kenogamie
sections.description:
Crazy fun, very long and pretty continuous spring run.

If you don't have a 4x4, you can also park on the 169 at the crossing of de pika creek, and then reach the Pikauba by water. Be careful with logs on the Pika.

This section is very remote and extraction would be like hell, so have a good team with you. This is not the place to lose a boat.

Be aware that there is a waterfall at half of the river. It starts with a R-IV that has no exit before dropping in the fall a couple hundred meters downstream. It is not obvious. For this reason, it is essential to be well prepared or to be guided on this river. Portage is on the right shore, juste before the R-4 turning right.

There is a second fall, 3 km from the first one. Scouting and portage is on the left.

Big wave party once you reach the convergence with the Aux Ecorces.

Take out is on the beach of the Sepaq (camping lac Kenogami)

sections.description: . Trick or Treat Easiest waterfall in the Ottawa area. Great access right off the side of the road with a short 100m walk. Excellent spot for beginners and the walk is about 20m from the bottom to the top so you can get dozens of laps in a few hours. If there is such a thing as a class II waterfall this is it. It's nice and deep you will not touch bottom even if you plug it. Height: 7 feet Runnable: March to December Access: Park on the shoulder of the road near 6833 Hwy 148 Quyon Quebec. Walk down the path near the river on the western side of the bridge.

Cette section recèle quelques belles vagues vertes à surfer en kayak de 8 pieds (et +) le long de la rive gauche. Attention de ne pas s’aventurer rive droite près de la jetée car il y a un énorme contre-courant turbulent à 400 m3/s. Il y a aussi d’énormes blocs de béton dans l’eau du centre à la rive droite avant et après la jetée. Voir carte pour ligne recommandée.\
\
Je préfère aussi rappeler qu’il y a un déversoir mortel à Chambly 4km plus bas. Donc si vous arrivez à la nage au pont de la route 112, lâchez votre kayak et nagez vers la rive la plus proche!

sections.name: Canyon
sections.description:
[French guidebook](https://qcrivers.blogspot.com/2007/10/le-canyon-de-la-rouge.html)

Guide for Canyon on the Rouge river

(Right) in this guide means river right.

You'll want to be a solid paddler that has done level 4 rapids with 2 others if you are doing this without someone who knows the river. This section is written in general for 100 to 130 CMS flows.

Also in general if you're not sure where to be in each section just keep middle right and on the harder stuff you'll want to be river right. On the easier stuff it doesn't matter.

The first succession of rapids are the hardest (Le Canyon, a 2 part rapid. You can scout the first set fairly easily and choose the best line for yourself you'll have a couple nice Eddie's before the second part of the rapid. The next rapid is the largest and you cannot scout this canyon run (R4).  You'll need to keep right and thread the needle or plow through the hole with a solid creek boat at levels 150ms/s or less. An angle wave/hole takes up the left and middle of the river, you can keep right and work your way around it. Then get ready of another reactive that pushes you left. The rest is straight forward. There is lots of water not many rocks at play in the large wave and it is not too retentive, it will push you out. Overall its an easy class 4. Similar to threading the needle on Makays or (Macoys) chutes on the Ottawa River just opposite (like if Phil's hole was before saddlers). Also no need to know this local route to understand the rapid here ( Phil's hole Saddlers etc) but it helps if you know it.

After is confusion rapid (rapids course de Cheval on Google maps) (R3)(500meters), there is no line here. It's a long rock garden  section at levels 130CMS and less. Keep the boat straight and dodge the rocks. You'll may find a play wave if you need want one.

Scie Ronde rapids are next R2-3.

At about 3km down river is Turbo rapid a solid R3. A couple large wave/holes push you left. Keep right for the easiest line.  Otherwise surf the waves out. It get a little boil on the way out.

The rest of the runs are straight forward.

A little after 3.5km into the trip you can keep left if you want the challenge of booking over a slightly rentenive hole. Not too retentive though my buddy got through it in a playboat at 115CMs

On the way out 7km) is a tight section called the Devils gates that gets a good boil in 150CM/s and above. Keep river right for easiest route. You'll notice the river narrow and a short canyon 50m high this is Devil's gate, the last part. There are no features here no waves rapids etc just a boil.

The rest of the runs are straight forward

*Ma Yo, 2022*

sections.name: Seuil Elisabeth
sections.description:
This section does not cover the waterfall section which is covered in a separate section.

Traditional putin is just below Esther's camping and parking lot.\
The first major rapid is call le Familial (class III, II when low).\
A sequence of flat areas and lesser rapids will take you to the first significant drop (Seuil Elisabeth), through a narrow channel and ending in a large pool. Following are some more flat, then increasing rapids, going through Slice and Dice) until you get to a sharp left turn to the start of a large canyon and the Washing Machine section.\
At normal levels and above, the water cover the left side of the Washing Machine drop - follow this to an easier and lower Drying Machine drop.\
\
Surprise follows with a drop passed just right of a big rock, then starts a long rock garden which ends in a lake with the New World takeout to the left. Pass this around a small lonely cone shape island of rock to enter the waterfall section. A first sharp turn to the left after the island, then follow the shore, river right, to the beginning of the waterfall portage. Climb a few feet to find the large foot path.

All beginners and intermediate will portage the long waterfall section on river right - a good 10 minutes walk.\
There is a fun seal launch to jump back in the river before the last drop.\
This last drop is portaged on the left. This last drop is often run over 70 cms at the far right through a narrow stairwell cascade.\
Others prefer to run that last drop on the left where there is a flake. Braver souls run it in the middle through chaos.

The following rapid - the naked's rapid / le rapide des tout nus - is a nice sustain rapid with a couple of surfing waves.

Takeout is at river left at la Halte des 7 Soeurs, above HW148, where there is a paying parking lot.

sections.name: White Dog
sections.description:
Starts at ABV. Parking has a fee.

There is a road all along the left bank.\
First big rapid is at Walker bridge (class IV/V) which is now closed to damage during the 2019 spring flood.\
For the normal Class III run, this rapid is portaged.\
The next main big rapid is the White Dog/Chien Blanc. White Dog is class III. The other rapids are class I or II.

You want to exit on the left bank before Table Rock rapid as this rapid flowing over bedrock has some strong hydraulics.\
Portage on the left side along the road and put in after the last drop of Table Rock, then paddle down another 500m to Esther's camping location where most likely you have your car parked. Parking there also has a fee, but members of the CCKEVM benefit from free parking (although not in 2020 due to Covid circonstances).

The White Dog rapid become a huge wave to surf between 430m3 to 560m3 on CEHQ gage. Solid paddlers can return in the wave via the eddy access, although most will get out high in the eddy and walk a few feet.

Attention à un undercut rive gauche sur la cascade de 5 mètres. 500 mètres de Boulder garden  read and run suive la deuxième cascade. La rivière se clame avec du classe II pour le 0,3 dernier kilomètre

sections.name: Polar Bear
sections.description: The gem of this section is the continuous rapid starting just ahead of the Constantineau bridge. Depending on levels the hole below that bridge can be retentive. Parking and access issues have plagued paddlers at that bridge, so an increasing number of paddlers are starting upstream, at Boyer bridge for instance, paddling a few warm up rapids before the Constantineau rapid. This continuous rapid is followed by mostly calm waters up until getting in sight of the Polar Bear spa. This rapid marks the most difficult part of the section. Paddlers will scout the rapid stepping out river left. At high level, the rapid develops a river wide hole right after the spa buildings on each side of the river. Boulders and holes complete the run under the highway bridge to a private land on the left.

sections.name: Lachine
sections.description:
The Lachine rapids offer a number of great standing waves for surfing, all located within the couple of hundred meters in the middle of the river. Big Joe/John and Maverick can crest at over 3m and the current is fast. A swim there is not recommended and may cause the poor sole to drift well over 500m. Best to go with a local who knows the path.

The waves are out in the middle of the St-Lawrence river, just below the Rapids Park and tricky to get to. \
Park your vehicle at the parking lot and walk up to the upstream bassin, paddle around the point and then keep your left.\
On the right, there are sieves and large boulders blocking above the main waves.

At normal levels, you start from a small island right after the severe S turn (col du cygne), located here; 45.428343, -73.584572\
At those normal levels, there is a network of little islands, shallows and ropes to get back to the starting island. At higher levels, the starting island goes under water along with remaining upstream path forcing paddlers to a one time surf.

It is the site of a local competition (MEV, <https://www.facebook.com/montrealeneauvive>) for surfing. They leverage jet-skis to transport and secure competitors during the 2 day event in late July now.

Keep some energy to ferry back to shore. You can ferry back to just below the Rapids Park beginning the ferry from the starting island (10+min ferry). Starting the ferry from the large eddy after the main waves will take you significantly down stream. You can then expect a good 15min to 30min walk back up to your car and the Rapids Park parking lot unless you've done a shuttle and left a car downstream.

*Note:* Swim not recommended!

sections.description:
Great surfing spot shared with many board surfers. Can be leveraged year around. The current typically prevents the ice from forming. The wave is right next to shore next to Place René Levesque off boulevard Lasalle, between Gagné and Raymond streets.

Wave is about 2-3 feet high and is a popular spot for surfers to hang out. Local school KSF.ca runs surfing class there. So the wave can be crowed.\
Putin is at the bench. Park on Lasalle.\
There are 2 waiting lines; one at the wave's eddy and one a bit upstream leveraged by novice surfers. The local etiquette calls to allow the next surfer to come from alternating lines.

Erosion is taking its toll and more and more of the shoreline is being eaten away.

sections.name: Parc Verendrye
sections.description:
La rivière Tomasine est située dans la région de l’Outaouais dans la partie sud-est de la réserve faunique de la Vérendrye. Elle prend sa source à l’extrémité sud du lac Embarras et coule vers le sud-est sur une distance de 25km avant de se jeter dans le lac Tomasine. De caractère très sauvage, elle offre une concentration impressionnante de rapides, seuils et chutes qui pourraient en faire un parcours sportif classique et magnifique avec l’aménagement de quelques courts sentiers de portage. Malheureusement, cette descente qui apparaissait autrefois sur la carte de la réserve faunique de la Vérendrye n’est pas entretenue et semble rarement parcourue. Les sentiers de portages y sont inexistants.

[Carte Plein Air](http://www.cartespleinair.org/Canot/04/TomasineDavidMarche2018.pdf) - David Marche

sections.name: Haute
sections.description:
This section can be combined with the Bras Louis sections.

This run offers lots of diversity with a continuous R3 4km straight canyon, small drops, 2m waterfall and nice R2 rapids for intermediate paddlers. This is a very remote location and hike out would be straining.

The trade of of this section is a pretty long shuttle, followed by a 30 minutes flat section on the bras Louis before joining the Valin. The run is about 2 hours but the overall shuttle time is about the same. This is why combining it with the Bras Louis make this completely worth.

sections.name: Section A
sections.description:
3 sweet rapids with a few bonus ones separated by a lot of flat water. The section finishes with a 25 foot waterfall. It's clean but has a class V rapid not far from it. At low flows, there's a large eddy between the drop and the class V rapid, however, take care at higher levels as a swim can lead your gear or even yourself down a pretty stout rapid!

**Water levels**

The online gauge is located at 3,5 km from the Saguenay entry.

ACCÈS À LA MISE À L'EAU DE LA MOYENNE VALIN (A)

Une entente, bien que précaire, a donc été conclue avec une riveraine pour donner aux membres du CKRS l’accès à la moyenne Valin. À l’entrée du boisée, nous avons installé une affiche puis des marqueurs à l’effigie du club afin d’identifier le sentier jusqu’à la rivière. Seul bémol, on ne nous a pas laissé installer de pancarte sur le bord de la route. Il faut donc vous fier au point, GPS affiché sur le google maps ainsi que de la photo prise de la rue. Voir les photos jointes à ce message. Votre capacité à transmettre ce message et votre civisme nous permettrons de préserver cette mise à l’eau.

ACCÈS CHUTE DE LA VALIN

Afin de préserver nos accès à cette merveilleuse section, le [CKRS ](https://www.facebook.com/ClubDeKayakRiviereAuxSables/)tient à remercier l’agriculteur qui a gentiment aménagé un parking pour kayakiste sur son terrain. En plus rester en contact avec le voisinage, nous avons installé une affiche avec quelques règles de civisme de base afin de pouvoir préserver cet accès. Le propriétaire du terrain sur lequel passe le sentier démarre la construction d’une nouvelle maison juste au côté de notre sentier ( vous avez peut-être remarqué les arbres abattus et la nouvelle entrée électrique). Il est en réflexion à savoir s’il préservera un corridor pour les kayakistes entre les deux maisons... On a donc intérêt à avoir un comportement exemplaire.

Voir carte et photos des sentiers dans l'onglet Media ci-contre.\
Pour tous commentaires ou questions, merci de contacter Kenjy, Xavier ou le [CKRS ](https://www.facebook.com/ClubDeKayakRiviereAuxSables/)(Club de Kayak de la rivière aux Sables)

sections.name: Wacouno
sections.description:
The Wacouno is an unique river that joins the Moisie rivers in its last 50Km. The section is 160km with about 100km of it being slow moving class I-II with flat water. It can take 5-7 days to cover this section. The shuttle is done with the train where you can ask to be dropped at the mile 100, it cost around 30$ (if you empty your boat).

In the first 40km you will have 2 canyons that are class IV-V-VI.  You can chose to portage each canyon by following the train rail. The days after that, constitute of relaxing on flat water with possibilities to do the historical hike, fishing, hike up the Dream catcher and much more. The last day the river flows into the Moisie where the last 5km are big water.

PS: Ce n'est pas la Rivière Nicolet, ce ruisseau (Waka) se déverse en aval dans le Ruisseau Demers et ensuite dans la Rivière des Vases.\

sections.name: B
sections.description:
La section est en deux parties. La première section est du R1. On se met à l'eau après le barrage.

On laisse les voitures stationnés à ce point (45.586504, -71.979739).

La première section finit dans un lac qui se termine lui-même avant un barrage. Il faut portager par les sentiers rive gauche.

Une fois le portage effectué, la deuxième section commence. C'est du r2 principalement.

Un beau trou se forme à partir de 1.4 sur l'échelle. À niveau 4 sur l'échelle, le trou devient plus un rouleau qu'un trou, mais les contrecourants devient plus petit sur les côtés de la vague.

L'échelle se trouve sur une roche que l'on observe à partir du pont du take out (45.571266, -71.993892)

Pour aller directement au trou sans faire toute la section, on peut remonter la rivière à partir du barrage du deuxième portage. À ce moment, stationne à l'aréna de hockey de Windsor ( 250 Rue Georges-Guilbault, Windsor, QC J1S 1M8).

sections.name: Willow Creek
sections.description:
Petit creek de printemps.\
Les pagayeurs démontrant des problèmes pourront sortir au pont du 11e rang avant de passer à une section plus difficile.\
Estimons II+ jusqu'au pont du 11e rang, III pour la suite.

Take out: petite entrée privée gazonnée, environ 3 autos. Pas de stationnement en bordure de rue 90kmh. SVP pas de déchets et éviter de flâner.\

sections.name: Basse
sections.description:
The classic run around the area.

Mostly continuous class 2-3 at normal water levels with one S-4.

Mostly run during spring time.

Runs from 20m3/s to 150 m3/s (highest I've seen it).

Garry is prime around 80m3/s for surfing.

sections.description: One of the classic run of the area. Mostly drop and pool class III/IV. Most rapids are worth scouting if nobody knows the section.
